proposals.title: Dissociating the Neural and Computational Signatures of Context Maintenance and Response Inhibition in Cognitive Control
proposals.motivation: Prominent models of cognitive control, such as the Dual Mechanisms of Control (DMC) framework, posit that proactive control is primarily implemented via context maintenance—the active representation of task rules and goals. However, recent work on the subjective costs of control suggests that the critical, effortful function may instead be the active suppression of prepotent responses. This proposal aims to directly test these competing accounts to clarify the core computational bottleneck in effortful control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in the subjective cost and neural implementation of cognitive control will be more strongly predicted by the efficiency of response inhibition mechanisms than by the fidelity of context maintenance.
proposals.method: We will employ a modified AX-CPT task with concurrent fMRI and pupillometry to index effort. Using precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamic causal modeling, DCM) of task-evoked activity, we will construct individual-level models of prefrontal-striatal circuits. We will fit these models to behavioral and neural data, explicitly comparing architectures where control costs arise from sustained context representation versus those where costs arise from transient, reactive inhibition. Model comparison will use Bayesian methods to identify the best-fitting architecture for each participant.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, model-based account of the primary source of control costs, moving beyond correlational approaches. It will refine theoretical models of proactive control and link individual differences in subjective effort to specific, dissociable neural computations within the fronto-striatal network.
proposals.novelty: While context maintenance is widely assumed to be the key proactive process, this study directly pits it against a reactive inhibition account using individual-level computational modeling of neural dynamics, a novel integration of Braver's DMC framework with cost-based and individual differences approaches.

proposals.title: Dissociating Causality in Cognitive Control: A Precision Modeling Test of the Epiphenomenal Conflict Hypothesis
proposals.motivation: A central debate in cognitive neuroscience is whether conflict-related activity in the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) serves as a causal control signal that recruits top-down regulation, or is merely a byproduct of processing dynamics. Pre-2026, correlational evidence dominates, leaving the causal role unresolved. Resolving this is critical for accurate models of control allocation and disorders of executive function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ACC conflict signals, particularly those correlating with error likelihood, are epiphenomenal to the implementation of cognitive control. Control adjustments are instead driven by dynamics in prefrontal and parietal regions, with ACC activity reflecting a readout of ongoing processing difficulty.
proposals.method: We will combine high-density EEG and fMRI during a flanker task with parametric difficulty manipulation. For each participant, we will fit precision data-driven models (e.g., dynamic causal modeling for fMRI, neural mass models for EEG) to estimate directed connectivity and latent dynamics. We will then use model-based causal analysis (e.g., Granger causality, state-space perturbation analysis) to test if trial-by-trial fluctuations in ACC conflict signals predict subsequent prefrontal dynamics and behavioral adjustments, or vice-versa.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, model-based test of causal directionality in the conflict-control loop. It will advance theories of control from correlational to causal mechanistic accounts. Furthermore, by leveraging individual differences in model parameters, it will explain why conflict signals show variable relationships with behavior across people.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying precision, individualized dynamical models—a recent strength of the Braver lab—to adjudicate a long-standing theoretical debate on causality, moving beyond standard regression approaches. This tests the non-obvious prediction that a robust neural signal is not a driver but a consequence of control.

proposals.title: Dissociating Error Likelihood and Subjective Effort Costs in Dorsal ACC: A Multi-Task, Computational fMRI Study
proposals.motivation: The dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) is implicated in both cognitive effort valuation and performance monitoring, but its precise computational role remains debated. Pre-2026 theories conflict on whether dACC activity primarily tracks objective error probability, subjective effort costs, or a combined signal. A systematic test is needed to map how dACC scales with objective task parameters versus subjective valuations across individuals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dACC BOLD activity will be parametrically modulated by objective, task-parameterized error likelihood across multiple cognitive domains, and that this signal will be dissociable from—and a primary driver of—individual differences in subjective effort cost encoding.
proposals.method: We will employ a within-subjects fMRI design with three tasks (Flanker, N-back, Task-Switching) where objective error probability is precisely manipulated via parametric difficulty staircases. We will use Drift-Diffusion Modeling (DDM) to derive trial-wise estimates of error likelihood. These estimates, along with subjective effort ratings, will be used as regressors in a precision data-driven GLM and dynamical systems analysis of dACC activity, building on the PI's recent modeling approaches to capture individual differences in cortical dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational account of dACC's role in error prediction, clarifying a core theoretical dispute. It will also establish a robust, multi-task paradigm for studying individual differences in performance monitoring and its relationship to effort-based decision-making circuits.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, within-subject dissociation of objective error likelihood from subjective cost using formal computational models across multiple tasks, moving beyond correlational approaches to test a causal, parameter-based account of dACC function.

proposals.title: Dissociable Functional Topography of Error and Conflict Monitoring within Human Anterior Cingulate Cortex Subdivisions
proposals.motivation: The anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) is central to cognitive control, but its functional heterogeneity remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 meta-analyses and theories suggest overlapping but separable roles for conflict and error monitoring, yet standard-resolution fMRI has been unable to resolve their precise anatomical loci. This ambiguity limits mechanistic models of control and its failure, which are critical for understanding individual differences in effort-based decision-making and psychopathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct, cytoarchitectonically defined ACC subregions (e.g., pgACC, aMCC/sACC) will show dissociable BOLD response profiles, with error-related activity localized more posteriorly/ventrally and conflict-related activity localized more anteriorly/dorsally.
proposals.method: We will collect high-resolution (e.g., 2mm isotropic) multiband fMRI data while participants perform a combined Flanker/Go-NoGo task designed to independently elicit conflict and error trials. Data will be analyzed using a precision data-driven approach, applying individualized anatomical parcellations of ACC based on the JuBrain cytoarchitectonic maps. We will use generalized linear mixed-effects models to test for subregion-by-condition interactions in BOLD activity, and dynamical systems modeling (e.g., using the HCP-MMP 1.0 atlas) to examine how these distinct signals propagate through resting-state networks.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, anatomically precise mapping of error vs. conflict signals in the human ACC, directly testing long-standing theoretical predictions. It will advance the field by linking specific cognitive control functions to their putative neural substrates, offering a refined framework for interpreting ACC activity in studies of cognitive effort and control costs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of high-resolution fMRI, individualized cytoarchitectonic mapping, and a task designed to dissociate signals within-subject, moving beyond coarse localization to test for a functional micro-architecture within the ACC that has been postulated but not conclusively demonstrated.

proposals.title: Single-Neuron Encoding of Effort Cost and Conflict Resolution in Primate Anterior Cingulate Cortex During a Stroop Analog Task
proposals.motivation: Human fMRI studies, including Dr. Braver's own work, have identified the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) as a key region for tracking the subjective cost of cognitive effort and for implementing control during conflict tasks like Stroop. However, the neural computations underlying these signals—whether they reflect integrated value signals, conflict monitoring, or effort-based action selection—remain poorly understood due to the limited temporal and spatial resolution of fMRI. Establishing a direct link between single-neuron dynamics and cognitive effort costs is a critical, unresolved question in cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single neurons in primate ACC will encode the relative subjective value of effortful, conflict-resolving actions, with distinct populations tracking conflict intensity, effort cost, and the integrated value signal guiding choice behavior.
proposals.method: We will train non-human primates on a color-word Stroop analog task where conflict level (congruent/incongruent) and effort cost (manipulated via reward magnitude or physical difficulty of the response) are independently varied. We will chronically implant multi-electrode arrays in ACC to record single-unit and local field potential activity. Neural data will be analyzed using generalized linear models (GLMs) and state-space modeling to dissociate signals related to conflict, effort, and value. We will apply the group-level data-driven dynamical modeling approaches from Dr. Braver's recent work to identify stable attractor states in ACC population activity during decision-making.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, mechanistic evidence for how effort costs and conflict are neurally represented at the single-cell level in ACC, bridging human neuroimaging and animal models. It will establish a causal, high-resolution foundation for interpreting dACC BOLD signals in terms of specific computational processes, significantly advancing models of cognitive control and effort-based decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While primate neurophysiology has studied ACC in reward and error processing, no study has systematically combined a well-validated conflict task (Stroop analog) with explicit effort cost manipulations to test a unified value-of-cognitive-effort hypothesis at the single-neuron level, as suggested by human fMRI findings.

proposals.title: Dissociating Conflict from Effort: Dorsal ACC Activity in Ultra-Fast Perceptual Decisions
proposals.motivation: The dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) is consistently linked to cognitive control and effort costs, but its precise computational role remains debated. Pre-2026 theories conflate conflict monitoring, subjective effort valuation, and adaptive control. A minimal paradigm is needed to isolate the core computational trigger for dACC engagement, separating it from downstream effort-related processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dACC conflict-related activity is triggered specifically by the simultaneous co-activation of competing response representations, even in the absence of overt effort costs, task difficulty, or explicit reward contingencies.
proposals.method: We will employ a rapid, two-alternative forced-choice perceptual decision task (e.g., motion direction discrimination) with parametrically varied levels of sensory evidence (coherence). Crucially, we will introduce conflict via spatially incompatible stimulus-response mappings (e.g., Simon task logic) orthogonal to perceptual difficulty. Using high-temporal-resolution fMRI (multiband acquisition) and EEG, we will model trial-by-trial dACC BOLD activity and theta-band oscillations with drift-diffusion models (DDM) to isolate the unique variance explained by conflict (drift rate vs. boundary separation) versus difficulty. Individual differences in dACC sensitivity to conflict will be related to resting-state network dynamics using precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamic causal modeling or recurrent neural network fits to resting-state data).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational, mechanistic account of dACC's most basic conflict detection function, clarifying its role as a precursor to effort-based valuation. It will establish a formal, model-based link between momentary conflict signals and individual differences in intrinsic brain dynamics, advancing towards a precision model of control allocation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using ultra-fast, low-effort decisions to strip away confounds of sustained effort and explicit cost-benefit trade-offs, testing if dACC conflict signals are a primitive building block of cognitive control, distinct from the subjective value of effort recently encoded in the same region.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Right Prefrontal Dynamics in the Integration of Sustained Attention, Detection, and Action Initiation
proposals.motivation: The right prefrontal cortex (PFC) is canonically linked to vigilance and sustained attention, but its precise computational role remains debated. Pre-2026 models often treat it as a monolithic 'alarm' system, potentially overlooking its integrative function in linking perceptual detection to action selection, a process central to understanding cognitive effort and control. This proposal challenges the narrow vigilance hypothesis by testing if right PFC activity dynamically encodes the subjective cost of maintaining readiness and the value of translating detection into action.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that right PFC activity patterns reflect an integrated, individual-specific computation that combines sustained attentional maintenance with the valuation of detection-to-action mappings, rather than pure vigilance. This integration will be evident in dynamical attractor states that are reliable within individuals but variable across them.
proposals.method: We will employ a novel cued sustained attention task with variable effort costs and probabilistic action outcomes, measuring behavior and fMRI. Using individual-differences approaches and precision data-driven modeling, we will fit dynamical systems models (e.g., Hopfield networks) to resting-state and task-evoked activity to identify subject-specific attractor landscapes in right PFC networks. We will then apply generalized encoding models to test if these landscapes represent the relative subjective value of effortful detection and action initiation, as derived from behavioral choice models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, model-based account of right PFC function that moves beyond the vigilance hypothesis to a framework of integrated detection-action valuation. It will establish a novel link between the dynamical systems approach to brain networks and the computational neuroscience of cognitive effort and control, explaining individual differences in sustained attention performance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in combining dynamical systems models of intrinsic brain architecture with trial-by-trial value encoding analyses to test an integrative (detection+action) role for right PFC, moving beyond canonical localization and revealing person-specific neural mechanisms.

proposals.title: Dissociating the Neural and Computational Signatures of Proactive and Reactive Control: A Precision Modeling Approach to Go/No-Go and Foreperiod Tasks
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is central to goal-directed behavior, but its subcomponents, like proactive (sustained preparation) and reactive (transient inhibition) control, are often conflated. The Go/No-Go and Foreperiod tasks are classic paradigms thought to differentially tax these components, yet recent theoretical work suggests they may engage overlapping processes. Resolving this ambiguity is critical for understanding the computational and neural architecture of control and its effort-related costs, a core theme in Dr. Braver's research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Go/No-Go and Foreperiod tasks, despite their differing surface structures, tap a common latent process of proactive temporal preparation, and that individual differences in the subjective cost of deploying this process will explain shared variance in neural activity and behavioral performance across both tasks.
proposals.method: We will collect high-density EEG and behavioral data from a large, phenotyped sample (N=150) performing both tasks. Using Dr. Braver's precision data-driven modeling framework, we will fit dynamical causal models (DCMs) to task-evoked and resting-state EEG to estimate subject-specific attractor landscapes in frontoparietal networks. We will then apply hierarchical Bayesian models to relate these neural dynamics to trial-by-trial estimates of subjective effort cost (derived from computational models of choice behavior) and trait-level measures of cognitive effort aversion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, model-based test of whether two foundational cognitive control tasks measure distinct constructs, directly informing theories of control fractionation. It will yield novel, individualized biomarkers of control-related effort costs, linking neural dynamics to subjective experience. The project will advance methods for studying individual differences in brain network dynamics underlying cognitive costs.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using precision dynamical modeling of EEG to directly compare the neural attractor states engaged by Go/No-Go and Foreperiod tasks, moving beyond descriptive activation comparisons to test a formal hypothesis of shared computational processes, grounded in the subjective value of control framework.

proposals.title: Dissociable Neural and Computational Signatures of Decisional Cost and Inhibitory Failure in Cognitive Control
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control impairments are central to numerous clinical conditions, but tasks like the stop-signal task conflate failures of proactive decision-making (e.g., to withhold a prepotent response) with failures of reactive motor inhibition. This conflation limits our understanding of the specific mechanisms underlying control deficits. Grounded in pre-2026 work on the subjective value of effort and individual differences in control, this proposal aims to directly dissociate these components to pinpoint the source of impairment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in cognitive control performance are primarily driven by variability in the subjective cost of making a proactive 'withhold' decision, rather than by the capacity for reactive inhibition. This decisional cost will be neurally encoded in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) and ventromedial prefrontal cortex (vmPFC).
proposals.method: We will design a novel fMRI task that orthogonally manipulates the requirement for a proactive withhold decision (via predictive cues) and the requirement for reactive inhibition (via stop signals). We will apply computational modeling (e.g., hierarchical drift-diffusion models) to separately estimate decision thresholds and inhibitory stop-signal reaction times (SSRT) for each participant. Precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamic causal modeling or latent factor analysis) of trial-by-trial BOLD activity, particularly in dACC and fronto-striatal circuits, will identify neural signatures uniquely predictive of decisional versus inhibitory parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational and neural dissociation between decisional and inhibitory components of control, moving beyond the unitary construct of 'inhibitory failure.' It will establish a novel behavioral and neuroimaging paradigm for precisely characterizing control deficits. The findings will offer a mechanistic framework for understanding heterogeneity in clinical populations and for developing targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied decision-making and inhibition separately, this proposal's novel task design and modeling approach explicitly and orthogonally dissociates them within a single framework, testing their unique contributions to control failures. This allows for a direct test of whether control costs are primarily decisional, an idea suggested but not conclusively proven by pre-2026 value-based models of effort.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Neural Dynamics and Subjective Effort Valuation Underpin Superior Cognitive Control Performance
proposals.motivation: Research on cognitive control has predominantly focused on deficits and average performance, leaving the mechanisms underlying superior control poorly understood. Pre-2026 work by Braver and others has established that the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) encodes the subjective cost of effort, and that resting-state brain dynamics show reliable individual differences. Studying high-performing individuals can reveal protective or enhancing factors that optimize the cost-benefit calculus of control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that superior cognitive controllers exhibit a unique neural signature characterized by (1) a reduced subjective neural valuation of effort costs in the dACC, and (2) more stable, idiosyncratic attractor landscapes in frontoparietal control networks that support efficient task-state transitions.
proposals.method: We will recruit high-performing and typical controllers using a validated, demanding cognitive control task battery (e.g., AX-CPT, N-back). Using fMRI, we will fit a generalized linear model (GLM) to quantify trial-by-trial dACC activity related to subjective effort valuation, as in Braver's prior work. Concurrently, we will apply precision data-driven dynamical systems modeling (e.g., using a Hopfield network or recurrent neural network architecture) to resting-state and task-based fMRI data to reconstruct individual-specific attractor landscapes. We will then use canonical correlation analysis to link these neural signatures to behavioral efficiency and self-reported effort.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond describing performance differences to identify the specific computational and neural mechanisms that facilitate superior control. It will establish a novel framework linking subjective effort economics with the stability of intrinsic brain dynamics, offering targets for interventions aimed at enhancing cognitive function.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied effort costs or neural dynamics separately, this proposal uniquely integrates these lines to test how idiosyncratic brain network stability may modulate the subjective cost of control, providing a mechanistic account of high performance rather than a mere behavioral description.

proposals.title: Distinct and Overlapping Neural Dynamics of Stimulus-Response, Response-Effect, and Cost-Benefit Rule Encoding
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is theorized to operate via different types of rules, from simple stimulus-response (SR) mappings to more complex response-effect (RE) and cost-benefit (CB) rules that incorporate outcomes and effort costs. Pre-2026 work has studied these in isolation, but a direct, within-subject comparison of their neural bases is lacking, limiting our understanding of how the brain integrates different informational dimensions for control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that while SR, RE, and CB rule encoding will share a common frontoparietal substrate, RE rules will uniquely engage outcome-prediction regions (e.g., ventral striatum), and CB rules will specifically recruit effort- and value-coding regions (e.g., dorsal anterior cingulate cortex, dACC), reflecting their distinct computational demands.
proposals.method: We will use a within-subject fMRI design where participants learn and apply three rule types in matched task contexts. We will employ individual-differences-based precision fMRI and dynamical systems modeling (e.g., using methods from Braver's recent work on attractor landscapes and data-driven cortical dynamics) to characterize rule-specific neural activity patterns and their temporal evolution. Multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and psychophysiological interaction (PPI) analyses will test for distinct neural representations and functional connectivity profiles.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated neural account of three fundamental rule types, clarifying how the brain represents action, outcome, and cost information. It will advance cognitive control theory by mapping specific computations to brain networks and offer a precision modeling framework for understanding individual differences in rule-guided behavior.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, controlled comparison of three rule types within a single paradigm and the application of advanced dynamical modeling to dissect their distinct neural dynamics, moving beyond localization to understand the computational mechanisms of rule representation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Energetic Cost of Cognitive Control: A Network Control Theory Model of Rule State Transitions
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is effortful, but the neural mechanisms translating subjective effort into the metabolic costs of reconfiguring brain networks are poorly understood. Pre-2026 work by Braver and others has linked the dorsal ACC to effort valuation and identified attractor-like dynamics in resting-state networks, yet a formal model linking network dynamics to the control energy required for cognitive transitions is lacking. This gap limits our ability to predict individual differences in effort-based decision-making from underlying neurobiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subjective cost of exerting cognitive control is proportional to the minimum control energy, derived from network control theory, required to transition the brain's dynamical system between distinct rule-representing states. Furthermore, we predict that individual differences in the estimated energetic cost of transitions will correlate with behavioral measures of effort avoidance and dorsal ACC activity related to subjective value.
proposals.method: We will apply network control theory to structural connectomes (from diffusion MRI) and regional BOLD time series (from task fMRI) of participants performing a rule-switching task. Using a linearized network dynamics model (A = -I + SC), we will compute the minimum control energy required to drive the system from baseline to task-engaged states defined by task-evoked activity patterns. Key algorithms include the calculation of optimal control inputs via the Gramian matrix and the modeling of state transitions as trajectories in a high-dimensional neural space. We will correlate these energy metrics with individual differences in effort discounting behavior and trial-by-trial BOLD activity in value-sensitive regions like the dorsal ACC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantifiable neuro-computational basis for the cost of cognitive control, moving beyond correlative measures. It will establish a direct link between the physical constraints of brain network dynamics and the subjective experience of mental effort. The resulting model will offer a new framework for understanding clinical conditions characterized by abnormal effort valuation, such as depression or schizophrenia.
proposals.novelty: While network control theory has been applied to brain states, its use to formally model the *energetic cost* of cognitive control transitions and link it directly to subjective effort valuation is novel. This integrates Braver's work on effort costs with cutting-edge dynamical systems approaches in a way not previously attempted.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Attractor Landscapes of Cognitive Control: Linking Excessive Rule Stability to Inflexibility in Obsessive-Compulsive and Autism Spectrum Traits
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is typically framed as a failure to maintain or implement rule representations. However, clinical observations in OCD and ASD suggest inflexibility can arise from overly rigid, persistent rule states. Pre-2026 computational work, including Dr. Braver's on attractor dynamics in resting-state networks, provides a framework to model this alternative pathology: cognitive inflexibility as excessive stability in neural attractor landscapes governing task rules.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in cognitive inflexibility, particularly in subclinical OCD and ASD traits, will be predicted not by weaker rule representations, but by excessive stability of these representations, quantified as deeper/more robust attractor basins in precision dynamical models of frontoparietal control circuits.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Hierarchical Gaussian Filter and precision dynamical modeling approaches from Braver's recent work. Participants (N=120, stratified by self-reported OCD/ASD traits) will perform a novel task-switching paradigm with variable reward for rule switching. We will fit individual-level dynamical models to task-based fMRI (dACC, dlPFC) to estimate attractor landscape parameters (e.g., basin depth, transition energy). These neural parameters will be linked to behavioral switch costs and subjective effort ratings for switching, using generalized linear mixed models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational account of pathological cognitive inflexibility, shifting the focus from deficient control to maladaptive hyper-stability. It will yield individual-difference biomarkers (attractor landscape features) for specific inflexibility profiles and test the role of subjective effort valuation (dACC) in maintaining overly stable states.
proposals.novelty: While existing models address control failures, this proposal uniquely applies dynamical systems theory to formalize and test the counterintuitive idea that excessive neural stability underlies specific forms of cognitive inflexibility, moving beyond a simple 'more control is better' framework.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Neural Signatures of Performance Likelihood: A Precision fMRI Study of Dorsal ACC and Frontoparietal Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on cognitive control has extensively studied error-likelihood signals, which reflect the brain's prediction of failure. However, the complementary neural signals predicting the likelihood of a *correct* response remain poorly characterized. Understanding this 'dual' signal is critical for a complete model of performance monitoring and for explaining individual differences in how people weigh the prospective costs and benefits of engaging cognitive effort.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed frontoparietal network, with a core hub in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC), encodes a dynamic, trial-by-trial signal of correct-response-likelihood (CRL), and that the strength and spatial topography of this signal will reliably vary across individuals, predicting their task engagement and effort-based decision-making.
proposals.method: We will adapt the cognitive effort discounting paradigm from Braver's prior work to include parametric difficulty manipulations. Using high-resolution, multi-echo fMRI, we will employ a precision data-driven modeling approach. We will fit dynamical systems models (e.g., Hopfield networks) to resting-state data to define individual-specific attractor landscapes. We will then use these individualized network models to constrain and interpret task-based general linear models (GLMs) and multivariate pattern analyses (MVPA) searching for CRL signals. Model comparison will test if CRL is a distinct construct from inverse error-likelihood.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the neural basis of correct-response-likelihood as a fundamental component of performance monitoring. It will extend the generalized encoding model of subjective value to the domain of performance confidence. Furthermore, by linking individual dynamical network properties to a specific control signal, it will provide a mechanistic account of why people differ in their propensity to engage effortful cognition.
proposals.novelty: While error-likelihood is well-studied, systematically searching for its inverse (CRL) as a potentially independent signal is novel. The approach is uniquely non-obvious in its integration of individual dynamical resting-state models with task fMRI to decode a prospective performance signal, moving beyond canonical group-level activation maps.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Network Hubs for Cognitive Control: A Precision Modeling Approach to Challenge the Canonical Role of Lateral PFC
proposals.motivation: Cognitive neuroscience has long posited the lateral prefrontal cortex (lPFC) as the central, obligatory neural node for cognitive control. However, pre-2026 work from the Braver lab and others highlights substantial individual differences in control-related brain activity and suggests a more distributed, systems-level architecture. This proposal directly tests the centrality assumption by investigating whether other regions, such as the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) or parietal cortex, serve as more critical network hubs for control processes in specific individuals or contexts, particularly those involving effort cost evaluation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the functional centrality of brain regions within the cognitive control network is not fixed to the lPFC but varies reliably across individuals, and that alternative hubs (e.g., dACC) will demonstrate greater predictive power for behavioral measures of effort-based decision-making and control allocation.
proposals.method: We will employ precision data-driven modeling of individual participants' fMRI data (resting-state and task-based from an effort-cost decision task). Using dynamical systems models (e.g., Hopfield networks or Kuramoto oscillators) fit to individual resting-state networks, we will derive person-specific attractor landscapes to identify putative control hubs. We will then apply graph-theoretic analyses (e.g., eigenvector centrality, betweenness) to task-evoked networks to quantify node centrality during effortful control. Multi-voxel pattern analysis (MVPA) and encoding models will test if activity patterns in these individualized central nodes better predict trial-by-trial subjective value of effort (as in Braver's prior dACC work) and control performance than lPFC activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, model-based challenge to the lPFC-centric view of cognitive control, demonstrating that control hubs are idiosyncratic and context-dependent. It will integrate the lab's themes of effort, control costs, and individual differences by linking personalized network architecture to computational variables of subjective effort value. The findings will shift the field towards a precision neuroscience framework for understanding control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond simply correlating lPFC activity with behavior to formally testing its network centrality against other regions using person-specific dynamical models, thereby directly evaluating a foundational but largely untested assumption in cognitive control research.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Breakdowns of Proactive and Reactive Control: A Precision Modeling Approach in ADHD and Schizophrenia
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Braver and others established the Dual Mechanisms of Control (DMC) framework, distinguishing proactive (sustained, preparatory) and reactive (transient, corrective) control modes. While these modes are dissociable in healthy individuals, it is unclear how they break down selectively in clinical disorders like ADHD (hypothesized proactive deficit) and schizophrenia (hypothesized reactive deficit). Understanding these selective impairments offers a powerful test of the DMC framework and a path to more precise, mechanistically informed clinical phenotyping.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ADHD will be characterized by a primary, quantifiable deficit in proactive control dynamics, while schizophrenia will show a primary deficit in reactive control, and that these deficits will manifest in distinct, individual-specific alterations in the attractor landscapes of frontoparietal control networks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the AX-CPT and other cognitive control tasks with concurrent fMRI to elicit proactive and reactive control. For each participant (N=120: 40 ADHD, 40 schizophrenia, 40 controls), we will fit precision data-driven dynamical models (e.g., using Hierarchical Gaussian Process Dynamical Systems or recurrent neural network architectures) to their task-evoked and resting-state activity, focusing on dorsal ACC and lateral PFC. Model fitting will estimate individual parameters governing the stability and switching between putative proactive and reactive attractor states within the network.
proposals.contribution: This work will move beyond syndrome-level descriptions to provide a computational and neural account of how specific control modes fail in major psychiatric disorders. It will generate testable, individual-difference predictions about control dysfunction and link them to reliable neural dynamical features, offering a novel taxonomy of cognitive control deficits grounded in systems neuroscience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying precision dynamical modeling—previously used by Braver to study canonical oscillations and resting-state attractors—to directly model the breakdown of control modes in clinical populations, moving from group averages to individualized mechanistic dysfunction.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Neural Signatures of Proactive-Reactive Control Interference: A Model-Based fMRI and Pupillometry Study
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is theorized to operate via proactive (sustained, preparatory) and reactive (transient, stimulus-driven) modes, but their neural and computational interaction under high demand is poorly understood. Pre-2026 work, including Braver's, shows these modes rely on distinct frontoparietal networks and have dissociable effort costs, yet paradigms isolating their simultaneous operation are lacking. A dual-task interference paradigm is critical to test if these systems compete for shared neural resources, creating a novel form of control cost that shapes individual differences in effort-based choice.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simultaneous demands for proactive and reactive control will generate behavioral interference and supra-additive neural conflict signals in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC), reflecting the integration of their subjective effort costs. Furthermore, we predict that individual differences in the neural dynamics of this interference will predict trait-level cognitive effort aversion.
proposals.method: We will design a novel dual-task fMRI paradigm combining a continuous performance AX-CPT (proactive control) with randomly interspersed Stroop trials (reactive control). Behavioral modeling will use a hierarchical Drift-Diffusion Model (DDM) to quantify interference effects on evidence accumulation. Neural data will be analyzed using precision data-driven modeling (e.g., Hidden Markov Models) of time-series data from dACC and frontoparietal networks to identify idiosyncratic dynamical states. Pupillometry will provide a continuous index of effort mobilization, correlated with model-derived conflict estimates and BOLD activity in the dACC.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first mechanistic account of how proactive and reactive control systems interact under conflict, formalizing a new dimension of cognitive effort. It will extend the generalized encoding model of effort value in the dACC to multi-demand scenarios. By linking individual differences in neural dynamics to effort-based decision-making, it will offer a computational bridge between cognitive control theories and motivational psychology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied proactive and reactive control in isolation, the novel dual-task design directly pits them against each other, creating a quantifiable 'control-interference' cost. The integration of precision dynamical modeling of fMRI data with formal cognitive models (DDM) to capture individual idiosyncrasies in this interference is a significant methodological advance beyond standard group-level contrasts.

proposals.title: Decomposing Task Boundaries: A Dynamical Systems and Computational Modeling Approach to Continuous Cognitive Component Flow
proposals.motivation: Cognitive neuroscience has traditionally relied on discrete task paradigms, which may impose artificial boundaries on the continuous, fluid nature of thought and obscure the dynamic interplay of core cognitive components (e.g., attention, control, valuation). This paradigm limits our understanding of how the brain allocates effort and manages control costs in naturalistic settings, a central theme in Dr. Braver's work on cognitive effort and control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that canonical cognitive tasks can be decomposed into a low-dimensional, continuous flow of latent cognitive components, and that individual differences in the dynamics of these components—specifically their stability and transition costs—predict subjective effort ratings and neural signatures of control in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC).
proposals.method: We will employ a novel continuous performance paradigm where stimuli and demands morph gradually between classic tasks (e.g., N-back, Stroop, task-switching). Using Dr. Braver's expertise in precision data-driven modeling, we will fit participant-specific dynamical systems models (e.g., recurrent neural networks or attractor models) to high-density EEG and/or fMRI activity to extract latent component trajectories. The relative subjective value of effort for different component transitions will be quantified via an effort discounting procedure, and its neural encoding will be tested against dACC activity using the generalized encoding framework from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel theoretical and methodological framework for studying cognition as a continuous process, moving beyond rigid task labels. It will directly link individual differences in the dynamical properties of latent cognitive components to the subjective and neural economics of cognitive effort, offering a more mechanistic account of control allocation.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct challenge to the discrete task paradigm by modeling the *continuous dynamics of cognitive components* within a single, morphing behavioral context, and in its integration of dynamical systems neural modeling with computational models of effort-based decision-making.

proposals.title: Causal Necessity of Frontoparietal Network Reconfiguration for Cognitive Control: A Combined TMS-fMRI and Computational Modeling Study
proposals.motivation: The frontoparietal network (FPN) is theorized as a flexible hub, dynamically reconfiguring its connectivity to implement cognitive control, such as during task switching. However, pre-2026 evidence is largely correlational, from fMRI studies showing FPN connectivity changes co-occur with performance. Establishing a causal link between FPN reconfiguration and control is critical for validating core theories of cognitive effort and control allocation, which posit that such reconfiguration is a key neural cost.
proposals.hypothesis: Transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) targeted to disrupt FPN node function (e.g., dorsolateral prefrontal cortex) will selectively impair task-switching performance by preventing the trial-by-trial reconfiguration of FPN connectivity with task-relevant networks, as measured by dynamic functional connectivity fMRI.
proposals.method: We will combine offline continuous theta-burst stimulation (cTBS) with subsequent fMRI during a cued task-switching paradigm. Participants will receive real or sham cTBS over left DLPFC, a key FPN node. fMRI data will be analyzed using established dynamic connectivity methods (e.g., sliding-window correlation, leading eigenvector dynamics analysis) to quantify FPN reconfiguration. We will apply precision data-driven modeling (e.g., Hidden Markov Models) to identify stimulation-induced changes in the temporal dynamics and attractor landscapes of network states. Individual differences in baseline control costs and network dynamics will be used as moderators.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct causal test of the flexible hub theory, moving beyond correlation. It will identify the specific dynamic connectivity signatures that are necessary for effective control. The findings will bridge Braver's work on the subjective costs of control with a mechanistic, network-level account of how those costs are neurally instantiated and disrupted.
proposals.novelty: While TMS-fMRI studies exist, none have specifically targeted and measured the causal necessity of rapid, trial-level FPN reconfiguration for control. The integration of perturbation, dynamic network analysis, and individual-differences modeling to test a precise computational hypothesis about network causality is novel.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Attractor Landscapes in Dorsal ACC Underlie Individual Differences in Proactive and Reactive Control Engagement
proposals.motivation: The Dual Mechanisms of Control (DMC) framework posits distinct proactive and reactive control modes, yet their neural implementation remains debated. Pre-2026 work, including Braver's own, shows the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) is critical for both effort valuation and control, suggesting a potential shared neural substrate. Resolving whether these modes rely on overlapping or segregated circuitry is essential for understanding the fundamental architecture of cognitive control and its costs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that proactive and reactive control are implemented by a common core circuit in the dACC, with individual differences in control strategy preference arising from person-specific dynamical attractor landscapes within this shared network.
proposals.method: We will combine the AX-CPT task (probing proactive/reactive control) with fMRI and computational modeling. For each participant, we will fit precision data-driven models (e.g., dynamic causal modeling or recurrent neural network architectures) to task-evoked and resting-state dACC activity. We will then apply dynamical systems analysis to these individualized models to extract low-dimensional attractor landscapes and quantify their stability properties in relation to behavioral measures of control engagement and subjective effort costs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, model-based account of how a core neural circuit can flexibly support two putatively distinct control modes. It will directly link individual differences in brain dynamics to behavioral control strategies, offering a novel explanation for variability in effort-based decision-making and cognitive performance.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond simple localization or correlation, this proposal tests the non-obvious idea that a single circuit's idiosyncratic dynamics, not its anatomical segregation, explains the phenomenological separation of proactive and reactive control, integrating Braver's recent themes of dynamics, individual differences, and effort costs.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Neural Attractor Landscapes Underlying Proactive and Reactive Cognitive Control
proposals.motivation: The Dual Mechanisms of Control (DMC) framework posits distinct proactive (sustained, preparatory) and reactive (transient, stimulus-driven) control modes, but their neural signatures are often conflated in group-averaged analyses. Pre-2026 work by Braver and others highlights the dorsal ACC's role in effort valuation and individual differences in control strategies, yet a precise, person-specific mapping of the dynamical neural systems supporting each mode is lacking. Defining these signatures is crucial for understanding the cognitive cost-benefit tradeoffs that determine control deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that proactive and reactive control are implemented by distinct, reliable neural attractor states within frontoparietal and cingulo-opercular networks, and that individual differences in the stability and energy landscape of these attractors predict behavioral efficiency and subjective effort costs.
proposals.method: We will employ the AX-CPT task to elicit proactive and reactive control within an fMRI session. Building on Braver's recent methods, we will use precision data-driven modeling of cortical dynamics via Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) and dynamic causal modeling (DCM) to identify transient brain states. We will then apply dynamical systems analysis (e.g., using the modeling approach from 'Dynamical models reveal...') to reconstruct individual-specific attractor landscapes from resting-state and task data, testing their reliability and linkage to behaviorally estimated control costs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, mechanistic account of control modes as neural dynamical systems, moving beyond correlational activation patterns. It will yield quantifiable, individual-difference markers (attractor stability, transition energy) that can explain variability in cognitive effort expenditure and control strategy preference, bridging the DMC framework with computational psychiatry and neuroeconomics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying formal dynamical systems theory to dissect control modes at the individual level, integrating task-based states with intrinsic network architectures to reveal the idiosyncratic neural 'physics' governing cognitive control decisions.

proposals.title: Micro-Longitudinal Dynamics of Cognitive Control: Idiosyncratic Shifts in Effort Valuation and Neural Attractor States Within a Single Task
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is theorized to involve dynamic shifts between modes (e.g., proactive vs. reactive), but these dynamics are typically inferred across trials or blocks, missing the moment-to-moment fluctuations that may underlie effort-based decision-making and performance variability. Pre-2026 work by Braver and others has established the dorsal ACC's role in encoding the subjective value of effort and the existence of reliable neural attractor landscapes, yet how these systems interact on a sub-trial timescale to govern control allocation is unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a single demanding cognitive task trial, individuals will exhibit rapid, idiosyncratic shifts between control modes, driven by moment-to-moment fluctuations in the subjective cost of effort, and that these shifts will be reflected in the destabilization and reconfiguration of attractor states within the frontoparietal control network.
proposals.method: We will employ a dense-sampling, within-subject fMRI design using a cognitively demanding adaptive task (e.g., a variable difficulty working memory or conflict task). We will apply precision data-driven modeling, specifically Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) and dynamic functional connectivity analyses, to high-temporal-resolution fMRI data to identify discrete neural states. These states will be linked to trial-phase-specific behavioral markers (e.g., reaction time variability, error likelihood) and modeled using a dynamical systems framework to quantify attractor landscape properties. The relative subjective value of cognitive effort will be estimated trial-by-trial using computational modeling of choice behavior derived from Braver's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first fine-grained map of how control states evolve within a task engagement, linking microscale neural dynamics to the computational valuation of effort. It will establish a novel framework for understanding intra-individual performance variability and offer mechanistic insights into why individuals differ in their susceptibility to cognitive fatigue or lapses in control.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has studied control modes across trials or tasks, this proposal uniquely focuses on sub-trial, second-to-second dynamics, directly testing the interplay between effort cost signals and neural attractor stability as a driver of moment-to-moment control decisions.

proposals.title: A Mandatory Gating Signal: Phasic Locus Coeruleus-Norepinephrine Reactivity Triggers Reflexive Cognitive Control Independent of Cost-Benefit Decisions
proposals.motivation: Prominent models posit that cognitive control is deployed via a cost-benefit decision, weighing effort against reward. However, rapid, adaptive control adjustments to errors or conflict appear too fast for deliberation. This proposal tests a complementary, non-decisional mechanism, leveraging pre-2026 advances in linking pupil-linked arousal to LC-NE activity and computational models of control to resolve a fundamental tension in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that phasic, stimulus-driven LC-NE reactivity, indexed by pupil dilation and the P3 event-related potential, acts as a mandatory gating signal that directly potentiates frontoparietal control networks, triggering reflexive control adjustments independent of slower, deliberative cost-benefit computations.
proposals.method: We will combine pharmacological manipulation (atomoxetine vs. placebo) with multimodal neuroimaging (MEG/EEG, fMRI) and pupillometry in a within-subject design. Participants will perform cognitive control tasks (e.g., flanker, Stroop) with embedded salient events (errors, high conflict). We will use Drift-Diffusion Modeling (DDM) to decompose reaction time distributions into decision and non-decision time components. Dynamic Causal Modeling (DCM) of MEG/EEG data will test causal interactions between the P3 generator network and frontoparietal regions, and how these are modulated by pupil-linked arousal and pharmacology.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic account of a fast, non-decisional pathway for cognitive control, directly testing its neural basis. It will advance precision models of cortical dynamics by incorporating a key neuromodulatory driver and explain individual differences in control efficiency as a function of LC-NE responsivity.
proposals.novelty: It directly challenges the assumption that all control deployment requires a cost-benefit decision by testing a reflexive gating mechanism. The integration of pharmacological perturbation with computational modeling of behavior and neural dynamics to dissect decisional from non-decisional control processes is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Dissociating Decision-Based from Habitual Cognitive Control: A Computational and Neural Signature Approach
proposals.motivation: A dominant framework in cognitive neuroscience posits that control deployment is governed by a cost-benefit decision process, as evidenced by value signals in regions like dorsal ACC. However, this view may be incomplete, failing to account for control that is deployed automatically due to learned task structures or individual predispositions, without an explicit valuation step. Testing this boundary is critical for a complete model of how the brain arbitrates between controlled and automatic processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant proportion of cognitive control deployment, especially in familiar contexts or for individuals with high trait control, occurs via habitual or policy-based mechanisms that bypass explicit effort-cost decision computations.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-session fMRI study combining a validated cognitive effort discounting task with a novel learned control task where control-demanding responses become habitual. Using Dr. Braver's precision data-driven modeling approach, we will fit both a hierarchical reinforcement learning model (with separate decision and habit systems) and dynamical systems models to trial-by-trial BOLD data. Model comparison and neural signature analyses will identify when and in which individuals (focusing on individual differences in proactive control) control engagement correlates with dorsal ACC value signals versus activity in sensorimotor or striatal habit circuits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal computational and neural dissociation between decision-based and habitual cognitive control, moving beyond a unitary cost-benefit framework. It will generate testable, individualized dynamical models of control deployment, explaining variance in behavior and neural activity that current decision models cannot capture.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly challenges a core tenet of the cost-of-control literature by formally modeling an alternative, non-decisional pathway. It innovatively applies Dr. Braver's methods in dynamical systems and individual differences to a new theoretical problem at the intersection of cognitive control and habit learning.

proposals.title: A Dynamical Systems Model of State-Dependent Cognitive Control Costs: Integrating Subjective, Physiological, and Neural Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Dominant models of effort-based decision-making, such as the Expected Value of Control, often assume a fixed or invariant cost for cognitive control. However, pre-2026 work by Braver and others shows that subjective effort valuation is represented in the dACC and that brain network dynamics are state-dependent. This assumption of a fixed cost fails to explain intra-individual variability in control allocation and motivation, limiting predictive power.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the subjective cost of cognitive control is a dynamic, state-dependent variable, determined by the real-time interaction of internal physiological resource availability (e.g., metabolic state, autonomic arousal) and external task demands, which can be formalized within a dynamical systems framework.
proposals.method: We will combine a cognitive effort discounting task with continuous physiological monitoring (pupillometry, heart rate variability) and a metabolic manipulation (glucose vs. placebo). Using precision data-driven modeling, we will fit a dynamical model where control cost is a latent variable evolving according to a differential equation driven by physiological inputs and prior control exertion. We will employ individual differences approaches to link model parameters (e.g., cost dynamics) to resting-state fMRI-derived attractor landscape stability in the frontoparietal and cingulo-opercular networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, testable model of dynamic control costs, moving beyond descriptive accounts. It will establish a direct link between moment-to-moment physiological states, idiosyncratic neural dynamics, and subjective effort valuation, offering a mechanistic explanation for fluctuations in motivation and self-control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in formally modeling control cost as a dynamic latent variable within a dynamical systems framework, rather than a static parameter, and in directly testing how this variable is shaped by the interaction of physiological resource pools and stable, individual-specific neural dynamics.

proposals.title: Modeling the Social Calibration of Cognitive Control: A Value-Based Neurocomputational Framework for Predicting Long-Term Trajectories
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in cognitive neuroscience has established that individuals weigh the subjective costs and benefits of exerting cognitive control, with the dorsal ACC playing a key role. However, these models are largely asocial, failing to account for how social environments and identities dynamically shape these cost-benefit calculations. Integrating social psychological constructs of motivation with neural models of control is critical for explaining real-world outcome divergence in education and careers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an individual's fronto-striatal control mechanisms are dynamically calibrated by social-contextual variables (e.g., regulatory focus, group identity), and that a model formalizing this calibration will outperform static neural or behavioral measures in predicting long-term academic and professional trajectories.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical reinforcement learning model where the value of control is modulated by social-contextual inputs (e.g., promotion vs. prevention focus) derived from ecological momentary assessments. Model parameters will be fit to individual fMRI data (multi-echo, precision imaging) during effortful tasks performed under different social primes. The fitted models will then be integrated into a dynamical systems framework to simulate longitudinal trajectories, which will be tested against real-world longitudinal outcome data using machine learning prediction models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal neurocomputational account of how social context embeds within control allocation circuits. It will yield a novel, predictive modeling toolkit for identifying individuals at risk for negative trajectories and critical periods for intervention, moving cognitive control research from the lab into the complexity of real life.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal integration of social psychology's nuanced motivational constructs into the core computations of a value-based cognitive control model, and in scaling this integrated model to generate testable, long-term dynamical predictions rather than explaining single-session behavior.

proposals.title: Modeling the Contextual Dynamics of Cognitive Control: A Neurocomputational Account of Effort Valuation and Implementation in Immersive Environments
proposals.motivation: Laboratory studies of cognitive control have established core principles of effort-based decision-making, often implicating regions like dorsal ACC in subjective effort valuation. However, these tasks lack the rich, dynamic contexts of real-world control deployment, limiting their ecological validity and generalizability. It remains unknown how domain-general valuation signals interact with context-specific control implementation processes when decisions are embedded in realistic, immersive scenarios.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in ecologically rich environments, neural signatures of control will decompose into a stable, domain-general component encoding the subjective cost of effort (as in discounting tasks) and a dynamic, context-specific component reflecting the implementation of control policies tailored to the immersive scenario.
proposals.method: We will integrate validated intertemporal/effort discounting tasks into immersive VR environments requiring sustained control. During concurrent fMRI and pupillometry, participants will make serial control decisions within these scenarios. We will apply a hierarchical Bayesian modeling framework, building on Dr. Braver's prior work with dynamical and precision models. This framework will use variational inference to decompose fMRI BOLD signals and pupil-linked arousal into latent variables representing a general effort cost and a context-bound control state, testing their trial-by-trial coupling to choice behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, testable neurocomputational model that directly links canonical effort discounting mechanisms to control implementation in realistic contexts. It will generate novel, individual-difference predictors of how people deploy control outside the lab, moving the field beyond simple task paradigms toward a more ecologically grounded theory.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the formal integration of validated computational tasks with immersive VR to create a 'best-of-both-worlds' paradigm, combined with a modeling approach that explicitly separates general valuation from context-specific implementation—a decomposition not yet attempted in ecologically valid settings.

proposals.title: Mapping the Neural Niche of Cognitive Effort Valuation: A Species Distribution Modeling Approach to fMRI
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is deployed based on a cost-benefit analysis, where the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) is implicated in encoding the subjective value of effort. However, pre-2026 work suggests such decision variables are represented in distributed, multivariate patterns rather than isolated regions. Current methods often fail to capture the full, idiosyncratic neural architecture supporting these latent variables, limiting predictive power across tasks and individuals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the neural representation of the subjective value of cognitive effort can be modeled as an ecological niche, where its expression across the brain is reliably predicted by a specific multivariate pattern of activity, and that this 'neural niche model' will reveal stable, generalizable, yet individually variable substrates.
proposals.method: We will adapt Maximum Entropy Species Distribution Modeling (MaxEnt) using the `maxnet` algorithm. fMRI data from an effort-based decision-making task will provide trial-wise BOLD activity (the 'environment') and a modeled subjective value of effort (the 'species' presence/abundance). The model will learn weights across all voxels to predict value magnitude, generating a whole-brain 'neural niche' map. We will validate model generalizability across task blocks and participants, linking individual differences in niche topography to behavioral measures of cognitive control.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel, predictive framework for mapping latent cognitive variables onto distributed neural patterns, moving beyond mere correlation. It will yield testable, individual-specific models of how effort valuation is neurally instantiated, directly informing theories of control allocation and its dysregulation in clinical populations.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual and methodological analogy from ecology, treating a cognitive variable as a species to be mapped across a neural landscape, which is distinct from standard multivariate decoding or functional connectivity analyses common in pre-2026 neuroscience.

proposals.title: Priming Motivational States to Dissociate Individual Differences in the Neural and Subjective Costs of Cognitive Control
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Braver's work, shows the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) encodes the subjective cost of cognitive effort, influencing control allocation. However, the field lacks a mechanistic understanding of how transient, context-sensitive shifts in motivation, distinct from stable traits, modulate these cost-benefit computations. Integrating social psychology's priming methods with neuroimaging can experimentally manipulate motivational states to probe this dynamic interplay.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that priming high-level motivational constructs (e.g., achievement, reward-seeking) will reduce behavioral and neural signatures of effort cost in a subsequent cognitive control task, and that the magnitude of this priming effect will be predicted by individual differences in baseline effort sensitivity and dACC responsivity.
proposals.method: We will adapt a validated cognitive effort discounting paradigm for fMRI. In a within-subject design, each scan session will include three blocks, each preceded by a different subliminal and supraliminal priming task (achievement, neutral, relaxation). We will use computational modeling of choice behavior to estimate trial-by-trial subjective effort costs. Neural data will be analyzed using precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamic causal modeling or latent dynamical systems approaches) to characterize how priming alters attractor-like dynamics in the frontoparietal control network and dACC-ventral striatum valuation circuits during effort-based decision-making.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel experimental paradigm for causally manipulating motivational states in cognitive neuroscience. It will provide a mechanistic, network-level account of how motivation transiently reconfigures control circuits, moving beyond correlational approaches. The findings will bridge social-cognitive and systems neuroscience theories of effort and control.
proposals.novelty: While priming is standard in social psychology, its application to directly manipulate the neural calculus of cognitive effort costs is novel. Combining this with individual-differences frameworks and dynamical systems analysis of fMRI data offers a unique, multi-level approach to a core problem in cognitive control.

proposals.title: Dynamic Threshold Policies in Motivated Control: A Hierarchical Bayesian Model Comparison Informed by Pupil-Linked Arousal
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is effortful, and motivation alters the speed-accuracy tradeoff, a process formalized by dynamic adjustments of decision thresholds in drift-diffusion models (DDMs). Pre-2026 work has proposed competing mechanisms for these dynamics—boundary collapse, shifts, or urgency-gated signals—but they have not been rigorously compared within a unified, biologically-informed framework. Resolving this is critical for a mechanistic understanding of how subjective value, effort costs, and arousal shape control allocation, linking Todd Braver's work on cognitive effort and control to its neural and computational substrates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that under motivated control demands, the dynamic adjustment of decision thresholds is best explained by a policy where an urgency signal, modulated by trial-by-trial pupil-linked arousal, multiplicatively gates the accumulation of evidence, rather than by simple boundary collapse or shift mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will fit hierarchical Bayesian DDMs to existing trial-level behavioral and pupil dilation data from motivated cognitive control tasks (e.g., the Demand Selection Task). We will implement and formally compare models with collapsing bounds, shifting starting points, and urgency-gated accumulation using state-of-the-art inference tools (e.g., PyMC3 or Stan with the HDDM extension). Model comparison will rely on cross-validated log-likelihood and WAIC, with pupil data directly informing trial-level parameters of the winning dynamic policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify the precise computational policy governing motivated speed-accuracy tradeoffs, moving beyond descriptive accounts to a mechanistic decomposition of control choices. It will provide a formal link between physiological arousal signals and dynamic decision processes, offering a novel, individualized model of how the brain implements costly control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, trial-level integration of physiology to arbitrate between competing computational models of threshold dynamics within a hierarchical Bayesian framework, moving past static DDMs to reveal the idiosyncratic, state-dependent policies suggested by Braver's recent precision modeling work.

proposals.title: Pre-trial neural microstates forecast within-person adjustments in cognitive control: A precision modeling approach
proposals.motivation: Cognitive control is dynamically adjusted based on factors like effort costs and motivation, but existing models primarily explain between-trial or between-person variance. The neural precursors of moment-to-moment control adjustments remain opaque, often treated as noise. This project addresses a core challenge in cognitive neuroscience: moving from post-hoc correlation to prospective, trial-level prediction of behavioral shifts by leveraging high-temporal-resolution neural data.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, rapidly evolving patterns of pre-trial neural activity (microstates) within a distributed motivation-control network (e.g., dACC, dlPFC, ventral striatum) contain reliable information that can be used to predict the direction and magnitude of subsequent cognitive control adjustments on a single-trial basis.
proposals.method: We will collect high-density EEG and pupillometry data from participants performing a validated cognitive effort task (e.g., the Demand Selection Task) with trial-by-trial measures of control adjustment (e.g., response time, accuracy, effort choice). Pre-trial microstates will be identified using data-driven methods like microstate analysis or Hidden Markov Modeling on source-localized EEG. We will then apply precision, individual-difference modeling techniques, such as dynamic causal modeling or idiographic multivariate pattern analysis, to build person-specific predictive models linking these microstate features to immediate behavioral outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel framework for predicting moment-to-moment behavioral variability from fleeting neural patterns, directly testing computational accounts of control allocation. It will provide a methodological advance by applying forecasting logic from complex systems science to neural data, offering new insights into the idiosyncratic, dynamic mechanisms underlying cognitive effort and control.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in treating pre-trial neural fluctuations as forecastable signals rather than noise and in applying a predictive, weather-forecasting analogy to within-person neural dynamics, which moves beyond traditional correlational or group-averaged approaches to cognitive control.

proposals.title: Dissecting Apathy and Fatigue: A Computational Phenotyping Framework for Latent Effort-Cost Valuation and Control Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Motivational deficits like apathy and fatigue are transdiagnostic symptoms across psychiatric and neurological disorders, but current diagnostic categories are descriptive and lack mechanistic specificity. Pre-2026 research, including Braver's work, suggests these symptoms may stem from altered subjective valuation of cognitive effort and failures in proactive control, yet competing computational accounts remain untested in a unified paradigm. A precise, model-based taxonomy is needed to move beyond symptom clusters and identify distinct pathological pathways for targeted intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct computational phenotypes—characterized by unique profiles of subjective effort cost, reward sensitivity, and prior beliefs about control—underlie the clinical presentations of apathy and fatigue. Further, these latent parameters will map onto dissociable neural dynamics in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) and frontoparietal control networks.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel effort-based decision-making task requiring graded cognitive control (e.g., N-back, task-switching) for variable rewards. Hierarchical Bayesian models (e.g., drift-diffusion or reinforcement learning models with effort discounting) will be fit to individual choice and RT data to estimate latent parameters. Concurrent fMRI (for dACC and network dynamics) and EEG (for trial-by-trial oscillatory power in theta/alpha bands) will be acquired during the task, with model parameters used as regressors for neural activity. Autonomic measures (pupillometry, heart rate) will index arousal/engagement.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a falsifiable, computational taxonomy of motivational deficits, linking specific parameter profiles to neural circuit dysfunction. It will provide a novel framework for identifying transdiagnostic mechanisms and generate individualized predictions for how different deficits (e.g., elevated effort cost vs. blunted reward sensitivity) respond to distinct interventions, advancing towards precision psychiatry.
proposals.novelty: The project's novelty lies in its direct, within-subject test of competing computational accounts of apathy/fatigue using a unified paradigm and its integration of individual-difference modeling with multimodal neural dynamics (fMRI attractor landscapes and EEG oscillations) to map latent parameters to specific, reliable neural mechanisms.

proposals.title: A Computational and Neural Account of Subjective Effort Valuation in Apathy, Fatigue, and Control Failure
proposals.motivation: Apathy, fatigue, and cognitive control failures are prevalent across psychiatric and neurological disorders, yet are often studied in isolation. Pre-2026 work, including Braver's on the dorsal ACC encoding subjective effort costs, suggests these states may share a core mechanism: a dysregulated valuation process where the expected costs of cognitive effort outweigh its perceived benefits. A systematic, model-based investigation of this shared valuation mechanism is critically needed to move beyond descriptive symptom categories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in the propensity for apathy, fatigue, and control failure are driven by a common latent factor: an idiosyncratically steep effort discounting function, neurally instantiated in the dynamics of the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) and its interactions with valuation and control networks.
proposals.method: We will combine a novel task probing effort-based decision-making and sustained control with fMRI and computational modeling. Participants will perform blocks of a demanding adaptive control task (like the AX-CPT), preceded by offers to exert high or low effort for variable rewards. We will fit hierarchical Bayesian models of effort discounting to choice data to estimate individual discounting parameters. fMRI data will be analyzed using precision data-driven modeling (e.g., Hidden Markov Models for dynamic network states) of resting-state and task-evoked activity to identify idiosyncratic dACC connectivity patterns. Dynamical systems analysis of these network states will test if control failure moments correspond to shifts into an attractor basin associated with low-effort valuation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified computational framework (an 'effort valuation model') for demotivational states, linking behavior to individual-specific neural dynamics.
