It will generate predictive biomarkers (model parameters and network features) that cut across diagnostic boundaries, offering new targets for interventions aimed at improving cognitive motivation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating Braver's research on effort cost encoding and idiosyncratic network dynamics to formally test a shared mechanism for three distinct clinical phenomena, using model-based fMRI to move from correlation to causation in understanding control failures.

proposals.title: Modeling the Control of Collaborative Neural Timing: A Multi-Agent Dynamical Systems Approach to Team Synchronization
proposals.motivation: Effective teamwork requires precise temporal coordination, yet the neural mechanisms that allow individuals to synchronize their internal cognitive timing for shared goals are poorly understood. Pre-2026 research by Braver and others has established the subjective cost of cognitive effort and the role of individual differences in control-related brain activity, but lacks a formal theory linking group-level incentives to the reconfiguration of individual neural oscillators for collaborative outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that group-level incentives and shared objectives act as control signals that modulate the coupling parameters of individual hippocampal-cerebellar timing loops, and that the emergent synchronization of this multi-agent neural system predicts the efficiency and performance of the collaborating team.
proposals.method: We will develop a control-theoretic model where each team member is represented as a Kuramoto-type oscillator, with intrinsic dynamics informed by individual differences in hippocampal-cerebellar resting-state connectivity (from fMRI). The shared goal is modeled as a target dynamical attractor, and group incentives as control inputs that adjust oscillator coupling strengths. We will fit and validate the model using precision data-driven techniques (e.g., Dynamic Causal Modeling) on neural (EEG/MEG) and behavioral data from dyads performing a collaborative timing task, testing predictions of synchronization stability and effort expenditure.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mechanistic, quantitative bridge from the neurocognitive basis of individual timing and effort costs to observable team performance. It will yield a novel computational framework for predicting how team dynamics emerge from and modulate individual brain states, with implications for optimizing collaborative environments.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates control theory with multi-agent neural oscillatory models to explain team synchronization, moving beyond individual cognitive control models to a formal theory of collaborative neurodynamics.

proposals.title: Dissociable Hippocampal Contributions to Learning Familiarity and Recollection: A Longitudinal, Closed-Loop Investigation of Recognition Memory Encoding
proposals.motivation: The dual-process theory of recognition memory posits distinct familiarity and recollection components, but the specific role of the hippocampus in *learning* these signals remains debated. Pre-2026 work often conflates hippocampal involvement in encoding versus retrieval or treats familiarity as a unitary, non-hippocampal process. A precise, mechanistic account of how hippocampal dynamics during learning differentially support subsequent familiarity-based versus recollection-based recognition is critically needed to resolve these theoretical disputes and understand memory deficits in disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the hippocampus is not necessary for the initial perceptual learning that establishes a stable familiarity signal for isolated objects, but its activity during encoding is essential for learning the contextual associations that later support recollection. This differential contribution will be reflected in distinct, dissociable hippocampal population dynamics during the encoding of items later recognized via familiarity versus recollection.
proposals.method: Mice will perform a continuous recognition task with objects of parametrically varied perceptual similarity, forcing reliance on either familiarity (high similarity) or recollection (low similarity with contextual change). We will conduct longitudinal Neuropixels recordings in dorsal CA1 and employ closed-loop, real-time optogenetic silencing triggered by specific task phases (initial object exposure). Population dynamics will be analyzed using precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamical systems analysis, latent factor models) to identify encoding-related activity patterns. Individual differences in these neural signatures will be related to behavioral performance metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, causal evidence dissociating the hippocampal role in learning familiarity versus recollection, moving beyond correlational studies. It will yield a computational model of how hippocampal population dynamics during encoding predict subsequent memory quality. The findings will refine dual-process theory by specifying the conditions under which the hippocampus is necessary for recognition memory learning.
proposals.novelty: The key innovation is the longitudinal, closed-loop design that isolates and compares hippocampal encoding dynamics specifically for items later recognized via distinct processes, rather than simply comparing remembered vs. forgotten items. This directly tests the learning hypothesis in a way that prior lesion or inactivation studies, which bluntly impair all hippocampal functions, could not.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Integration of Hippocampal and Cerebellar Timing Signals Predicts Individual Differences in Effortful Temporal Control
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 models of interval timing often propose a modular architecture, with the hippocampus supporting episodic, memory-based timing and the cerebellum supporting automatic, sub-second motor timing. However, real-world cognitive control often requires the integration of these distinct temporal scales under conditions of high effort. The degree to which these systems interact in a fixed versus a flexible, individualized manner remains unknown, limiting our understanding of the neural basis of temporal decision-making and effort costs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hippocampal and cerebellar timing mechanisms are not strictly separable but are dynamically integrated during cognitively effortful timing tasks, and that the strength and pattern of this integration are reliable, idiosyncratic neural signatures that predict individual differences in subjective effort costs and behavioral performance.
proposals.method: We will employ a precision data-driven modeling approach, combining fMRI with a novel effort-based temporal reproduction task requiring integration of supra- and sub-second intervals. Using Dynamical Causal Modeling (DCM) and connectome-based predictive modeling, we will estimate directed effective connectivity between hippocampal and cerebellar regions. We will then apply hierarchical Bayesian models to relate individual-specific connectivity profiles to behavioral measures of effort cost (derived from effort-discounting choices) and performance variability, testing for reliable individual differences in network configuration.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond modular timing models by providing a formal, mechanistic account of how major timing systems interact under cognitive control demands. It will establish a novel framework linking individual differences in large-scale temporal network dynamics to the subjective valuation of cognitive effort, a core concept in Dr. Braver's research program.
proposals.novelty: The proposal is novel in testing for *idiosyncratic but reliable* integration patterns between hippocampus and cerebellum, challenging the assumption of a universal modular architecture, and directly connecting these neural dynamics to computational models of effort-based decision-making.

proposals.title: A Computational Dissociation of Hippocampal Control vs. Encoding Architectures in Spatial Navigation: Model Predictions for Neural Dynamics and Effortful Reorientation
proposals.motivation: The hippocampus is central to spatial navigation, but its computational role remains debated. Pre-2026 theories conflict on whether it directly encodes cognitive maps (place/grid cells) or exerts top-down control over path integration in entorhinal and parietal circuits, especially during effortful reorientation. Resolving this is critical for understanding how cognitive control mechanisms, a core theme of Dr. Braver's work, are implemented in allocentric spatial processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hippocampal control model, where CA1 outputs modulate the gain and error-correction of entorhinal path integrators, will produce distinct neural dynamics and behavioral signatures—particularly under sensory conflict or high cognitive effort—compared to a classical direct encoding model.
proposals.method: We will develop two biologically constrained recurrent neural network (RNN) models within identical virtual navigation tasks. The 'Control' model will implement the hippocampus as a dynamical controller that updates attractor landscapes in entorhinal grid cell networks, using precision data-driven modeling akin to recent work. The 'Encoding' model will use a classic place-cell-centric architecture. We will simulate individual differences by varying model parameters (e.g., control strength) and compare outputs using Dr. Braver's methods for analyzing neural population dynamics (e.g., replay sequences, error signals) and behavioral costs during path integration.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel set of empirically testable predictions (e.g., specific LFP signatures during conflict, correlations between control signal magnitude and behavioral robustness) to distinguish these mechanistic accounts. It will formally integrate concepts of cognitive control and effort costs into spatial navigation models, offering a new framework for interpreting hippocampal activity beyond pure representation.
proposals.novelty: While models exist for both encoding and control, no pre-2026 work has directly and quantitatively pitted them against each other in identical tasks to derive falsifiable signatures in both neural dynamics and effort-related behavior, leveraging individual differences in control propensity.

proposals.title: A Double Dissociation Framework for Causal Benchmarking of Cognitive Models: Testing Hippocampal Engagement in Episodic Memory
proposals.motivation: Computational models of memory often achieve similar fits to behavioral data despite proposing fundamentally different cognitive architectures, such as whether the hippocampus is essential for all episodic encoding or if neocortical routes can suffice. Pre-2026 model comparison relies heavily on correlational metrics like goodness-of-fit, which cannot adjudicate between these architectures or establish causal necessity. A principled, causal testing framework is needed to move beyond descriptive fits and toward falsifiable architectural theories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a formal double dissociation framework—using targeted computational perturbations—will reveal a clean, dissociable pattern of deficits across a benchmark suite, providing causal evidence that distinguishes models positing obligatory hippocampal encoding from those proposing direct neocortical routes.
proposals.method: We will implement a benchmark suite of tasks (e.g., pattern separation, proactive interference, rapid episodic binding) within a unified simulation environment. We will then apply targeted perturbations (e.g., simulated hippocampal lesions, noise injection into specific pathways, parameter manipulations affecting effortful control) to two competing model classes: one with obligatory hippocampal engagement and one with a direct neocortical encoding route. Performance will be analyzed using dynamical systems and precision modeling techniques to identify attractor landscapes and robustness, creating a formal dissociation matrix.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, causal benchmarking methodology for computational cognitive neuroscience, moving beyond model fitting to architectural testing. It will yield specific, falsifiable predictions about the functional roles of brain regions like the dACC (in effortful control) and hippocampus, grounded in individual differences in model robustness.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in formally adapting the causal logic of neuropsychological double dissociation to purely computational model comparison, creating a rigorous test of architectural assumptions that standard model comparison lacks.

proposals.title: Cognitive Effort Valuation Without Direct Hippocampal Encoding: A Computational and Neural Comparison of Model Architectures
proposals.motivation: A central debate in cognitive control and effort-based decision-making concerns the neural mechanisms for representing the prospective cost of demanding tasks. Pre-2026 work, including Braver's on dorsal ACC encoding of effort value, suggests a valuation system largely independent of episodic memory. However, alternative models propose that the hippocampus directly encodes future cognitive effort costs, integrating them into episodic simulations. Resolving this architectural distinction is critical for understanding individual differences in control allocation and effort avoidance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a model where cognitive effort valuation is computed via prefrontal-striatal circuits, without direct hippocampal encoding, will better account for both behavioral choice patterns and trial-to-trial neural dynamics in the dorsal ACC during effort-based decision-making, compared to models incorporating direct hippocampal encoding of effort costs.
proposals.method: We will fit and compare three computational architectures to behavioral and fMRI data from an established cognitive effort discounting task: 1) a prefrontal-striatal model (based on Braver's prior work), 2) a hippocampal-dependent model where effort costs are embedded in episodic future simulations, and 3) a hybrid model. Model comparison will use hierarchical Bayesian fitting and precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamic causal modeling or recurrent neural network models) of trial-level BOLD activity in dorsal ACC, hippocampus, and ventromedial PFC. Individual differences in model parameters will be linked to trait measures of cognitive effort avoidance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive computational test of competing neural architectures for effort valuation, clarifying the hippocampus's role. It will yield a formal, empirically validated model of how the brain estimates cognitive costs, advancing theories of control allocation. Furthermore, it will identify individual difference profiles linked to specific valuation mechanisms, with implications for understanding motivational deficits.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has identified key brain regions, this proposal uniquely pits formal, whole-brain dynamical models against each other on the specific question of hippocampal necessity. The integration of precision data-driven cortical dynamics modeling with established cognitive effort discounting paradigms to dissect model architectures is a novel synthesis.

proposals.title: Dynamical Systems Analysis of the Effortful Control of Declarative-Procedural Memory Interactions
proposals.motivation: Declarative and procedural memory systems are known to interact competitively and cooperatively, but these interactions are typically studied as static or sequential. Understanding them as a continuous, coupled dynamical system is crucial for explaining real-time cognitive control and effort allocation during learning. This approach directly addresses the core themes of cognitive effort and control costs by modeling how the brain manages the trade-off between these systems under varying task demands.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the relative engagement of declarative versus procedural memory systems during a dual-task paradigm can be modeled as a low-dimensional attractor landscape, where cognitive effort modulates the stability of attractor states and the energy barriers between them.
proposals.method: We will adapt a probabilistic classification task known to engage both memory systems, collecting high-density EEG and pupilometry (as a proxy for effort) from a large individual differences sample. Using pre-2026 dynamical systems tools, we will apply a data-driven dimensionality reduction (e.g., Laplacian Eigenmaps) to neural activity to identify latent states. We will then fit a drift-diffusion model to characterize the attractor dynamics, with model parameters (e.g., drift rates, potential well depths) estimated from individual participants' data and linked to effort-related physiological and behavioral measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, mechanistic model of how cognitive control effort shapes the dynamic interplay between major memory systems. It will generate novel, individual-specific biomarkers of control efficiency and effort costs, extending the lab's work on the dorsal ACC's role in effort valuation to the domain of memory system competition.
proposals.novelty: While dynamical systems analyses have been applied to resting-state networks and cortical oscillations, applying this framework to model the real-time, effort-dependent competition between two well-defined long-term memory systems is a novel synthesis. The integration of individual differences in effort sensitivity with the formal analysis of memory system dynamics is a distinct advance.

proposals.title: Low-Frequency Conflict Monitoring in Complex Cognition: An Individual-Differences Approach to Planning and Reasoning
proposals.motivation: The conflict monitoring hypothesis, centered on the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), has been primarily tested in simple, rapid-response tasks (e.g., Stroop, flanker). However, real-world cognitive control involves managing conflict in complex, multi-step tasks like planning and reasoning, where conflict may unfold over longer timescales. Extending the low-frequency conflict hypothesis to these domains is crucial for a general theory of cognitive effort and control, bridging the gap between canonical paradigms and higher-order cognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that low-frequency (<0.1 Hz) neural dynamics, particularly in the dorsal ACC and frontoparietal networks, will track the magnitude of conflict and subjective effort costs in complex planning and reasoning tasks, and that individual differences in these neural signatures will predict behavioral performance and self-reported effort.
proposals.method: We will employ a within-subjects fMRI design using a validated complex task (e.g., the Tower of London for planning, or Raven's-like matrix reasoning). We will use precision data-driven modeling, specifically applying dynamical systems analysis (e.g., using the HMM-MAR toolbox) to resting-state and task-based fMRI to identify idiosyncratic, low-frequency attractor landscapes. We will then test if task-evoked activity in these individualized networks tracks trial-by-trial conflict (quantified via computational model-derived conflict signals) and subjective value of effort (using an effort-discounting paradigm).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish whether the neural principles of conflict monitoring generalize to complex cognition, providing a mechanistic link between low-frequency cortical dynamics and the subjective costs of high-level mental effort. It will also deliver a novel, individualized framework for modeling control-related brain activity that moves beyond canonical group-level activation maps.
proposals.novelty: While conflict monitoring is well-studied, applying its core neural hypothesis to the slow, evolving conflict of planning/reasoning tasks is novel. Furthermore, integrating individualized dynamical network models with trial-level conflict and effort valuation metrics represents a significant methodological advance over standard GLM approaches.

proposals.title: High-Temporal Resolution Mapping of Low-Frequency Conflict Dynamics: An EEG/MEG Replication and Extension of Dorsal ACC Value-of-Effort Signals
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 fMRI work by Braver and others has identified a low-frequency (<0.1 Hz) conflict signal in dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) that tracks the subjective cost of cognitive effort. However, fMRI's poor temporal resolution obscures the rapid neural dynamics of conflict monitoring and effort valuation, limiting mechanistic understanding of how these signals evolve and influence control allocation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the low-frequency conflict/effort-cost signal previously observed in dACC fMRI will be detectable with high temporal precision using EEG/MEG source imaging, manifesting as a slow cortical potential or power modulation in the theta-alpha band (4-12 Hz) that scales with subjective effort cost and predicts subsequent behavioral adjustments.
proposals.method: We will collect simultaneous EEG-fMRI (or MEG) data from 50 participants performing the cognitive effort discounting task used in Braver's prior work. We will apply individual differences approaches, using each participant's fMRI-derived dACC region-of-interest to constrain EEG/MEG source reconstruction. Dynamical models and precision data-driven techniques (e.g., Hidden Markov Models, multivariate pattern analysis on time-frequency representations) will be used to identify the temporal evolution and oscillatory signatures of the effort-value signal and its relationship to behaviorally estimated effort costs.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, millisecond-scale characterization of the neural dynamics underlying effort-based decision-making in dACC, bridging the gap between hemodynamic and electrophysiological markers of cognitive control. It will validate a core fMRI finding with a complementary modality, strengthening the theoretical foundation, and reveal the idiosyncratic temporal profiles of these signals across individuals.
proposals.novelty: While conflict-related EEG signals like the N2/ERN are well-studied, directly mapping the specific, low-frequency effort-valuation signal from fMRI to electrophysiology with individual anatomical precision is novel. The integration of individual fMRI localizers with dynamical modeling of EEG/MEG to capture subject-specific temporal dynamics of effort cost represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Subjective Cost of Cognitive Control: A Hierarchical Drift-Diffusion Model of Effort-Based Decision-Making Linked to Dorsal ACC Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) is consistently implicated in effort-based decision-making and cognitive control, but its precise computational role remains debated. Pre-2026 work, including Braver's own, suggests the dACC encodes the relative subjective value of cognitive effort, yet a formal, mechanistic link between the computational processes of effort valuation and the neural signals observed in fMRI is lacking. Bridging this gap is critical for a quantitative, process-level understanding of how control costs are evaluated and guide behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the drift rate parameter of a hierarchical drift-diffusion model (HDDM), fitted to choices about whether to engage in effortful cognitive tasks, will directly quantify the subjective cost of control on a trial-by-trial basis, and that this trial-wise cost signal will be parametrically encoded in dACC BOLD activity.
proposals.method: We will adapt an established effort-based decision task where participants choose between a high-effort/high-reward and a low-effort/low-reward option. We will fit a hierarchical Bayesian drift-diffusion model (HDDM) to the choice and reaction time data, treating the drift rate as a function of the objective effort cost and individual subjective cost sensitivity. Using trial-wise estimates of the drift rate (the latent decision variable), we will perform model-based fMRI analysis to test for parametric modulation of dACC BOLD activity by this computationally derived cost signal. We will also examine individual differences, linking subject-specific HDDM parameters to resting-state network dynamics and task-evoked dACC responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal computational account of how subjective effort costs are dynamically integrated during decision-making, moving beyond correlational neural signals. It will establish a direct link between a core parameter of a well-validated decision model and dACC function, offering a novel, quantitative biomarker for the cost of control. The findings will advance theories of cognitive control by grounding them in the mechanistic framework of sequential sampling models.
proposals.novelty: While DDM has been extensively used in perceptual decision-making, its application to model the *subjective valuation of cognitive effort* in a choice paradigm is novel. Furthermore, linking the trial-varying drift rate, rather than just choice outcomes or difficulty, to dACC fMRI signals provides a unique, process-oriented neural correlate of cost accumulation.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Pupillometric Signatures of Cognitive Conflict: Linking Dynamic Attractor Models to Individual Differences in Effort Cost
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that pupil dilation is a sensitive, real-time index of cognitive effort and locus coeruleus-norepinephrine (LC-NE) system activity, often linked to task engagement and conflict. However, its relationship to the specific computational dynamics of conflict processing, as conceptualized in models of cortical attractor landscapes, remains underspecified. This gap limits the use of pupillometry as a precise, non-imaging proxy for the subjective cost of control, a central theme in Dr. Braver's work on effort-based decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that trial-by-trial fluctuations in pupil diameter during low-frequency conflict events (e.g., incongruent Stroop trials) will reflect the dynamical properties of an individual's cortical conflict attractor network, and that individual differences in the gain and temporal profile of this pupillary response will predict behavioral and neural markers of subjective effort cost.
proposals.method: Participants will perform a parametric conflict task (e.g., a graded Stroop or Simon task) while high-resolution pupillometry and fMRI data are collected concurrently. We will apply Dr. Braver's established precision data-driven modeling pipeline to fMRI data, fitting dynamical models (e.g., Hopfield networks) to estimate individual-specific attractor landscape properties in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) and frontoparietal network. Pupillometry time-series will be decomposed using deconvolution algorithms (e.g., based on the LC-NE response function) to isolate conflict-related dilation. We will then use canonical correlation analysis and hierarchical Bayesian modeling to link individual landscape stability metrics (e.g., settling time, basin depth) to parameters of the pupillary response (amplitude, latency, sustained component).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a formal, model-based link between a non-invasive, continuous physiological measure (pupillometry) and the latent dynamics of cortical conflict processing. It will provide a novel, accessible method for estimating individual differences in the neural instantiation of control costs, extending the generalized encoding framework from the dACC to the peripheral autonomic system. The findings will validate pupillometry as a robust proxy for computationally defined conflict, enabling future high-throughput or ecological studies of effort-based decision-making.
proposals.novelty: While pupillometry has been linked to effort and arousal, this proposal uniquely tests its direct correspondence with *model-derived* cortical dynamics of conflict, moving beyond correlation with behavior or BOLD amplitude. The integration of precision fMRI modeling with pupillometry to capture individual idiosyncrasies in a control-related cost signal is a novel synthesis of Dr. Braver's recent methodological themes.

proposals.title: Causal Disruption of Dorsal ACC Low-Frequency Dynamics Impairs the Subjective Valuation of Cognitive Effort
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Braver and others has established the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) as a key region for encoding the subjective cost of cognitive effort, with its low-frequency (<0.1 Hz) dynamics being critical for conflict processing and control allocation. However, these findings are primarily correlational, derived from fMRI and computational modeling. A direct causal test is required to determine if dACC activity mechanistically drives the valuation process that underlies effort-based decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that theta-burst transcranial magnetic stimulation (TBS) applied to the dACC will selectively disrupt its low-frequency oscillatory dynamics, leading to a behavioral reduction in the sensitivity to cognitive effort costs, as reflected in altered choice patterns on an effort discounting task.
proposals.method: We will employ a within-subjects, sham-controlled TMS design. Individualized dACC targets will be identified using each participant's structural and resting-state fMRI data, focusing on the functional connectivity profile associated with effort valuation from Braver's prior work. Continuous theta-burst stimulation (cTBS) will be applied to transiently inhibit the target. Participants will then perform a well-validated cognitive effort discounting task (like the Demand Selection Task) where they choose between high-effort/high-reward and low-effort/low-reward options. We will use hierarchical Bayesian parameter estimation of a drift-diffusion model to quantify trial-by-trial effort sensitivity (k-effort parameter) and decision dynamics post-stimulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first causal evidence linking dACC physiology, specifically its low-frequency dynamics, to the computational process of effort cost valuation. It will bridge Braver's programmatic research on individual differences in control, effort costs, and dynamical network models by directly manipulating the proposed neural mechanism. The findings will constrain future computational models of cognitive control by establishing necessary neural components.
proposals.novelty: While TMS has been applied to frontal control regions, no study has yet combined individualized dACC targeting based on effort-valuation networks with a precise computational model of choice behavior to test causality. This moves beyond simply disrupting performance to testing a specific mechanistic hypothesis about how low-frequency dynamics shape subjective value signals.

proposals.title: Dorsal ACC Activity and the Subjective Value of Cognitive Effort in States of High-Frequency Behavioral Dominance
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Braver's own work, has established the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) as a key region for encoding the subjective cost of cognitive effort, particularly during tasks requiring control to overcome conflict or prepotent responses. However, the field lacks a principled understanding of dACC dynamics when the prepotent response is correct and executed fluently, a state of 'high-frequency dominance.' This gap limits a complete model of how the brain evaluates effort costs and allocates control in ecologically valid scenarios where automatic behaviors are often adaptive.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that during high-frequency dominance states, dACC activity will inversely correlate with the subjective value of cognitive effort, reflecting a lower cost estimate for the prepotent action, and that individual differences in this neural signature will predict trait-level sensitivity to effort costs.
proposals.method: We will employ a validated cognitive effort discounting task where the high-frequency, prepotent response is the correct choice on a majority of trials. Concurrent fMRI will measure dACC BOLD activity. We will use precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamic causal modeling or a hierarchical Bayesian framework) to characterize the attractor landscape of dACC-prefrontal circuits during these states. Individual difference measures of cognitive effort avoidance will be used to constrain models and test for neural-behavioral correlations.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first neural evidence for how the dACC's effort valuation system operates in the absence of response conflict, testing the generality of current models. It will yield a more complete, dynamical systems account of cognitive control that explains both conflict-driven and fluency-driven modes of operation. The findings will bridge theories of effort-based decision-making with models of automaticity and habit.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classic conflict-monitoring framework to study control system dynamics when control is *not* urgently needed, a largely neglected but common brain state. It integrates Braver's recent work on effort valuation, dynamical systems, and individual differences to test a novel, counter-intuitive prediction about dACC function.

proposals.title: Dissociating the Neural Signatures of Perceptual Conflict Valuation and Motor Response Conflict in the Anterior Cingulate Cortex
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including from Dr. Braver's lab, has established the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC) as a key region for encoding the subjective value and expected costs of cognitive effort, often during tasks requiring effortful control over action. However, it remains unclear whether dACC activity specifically tracks the cost of resolving response conflict, or if it more generally signals the cost of resolving any high-level conflict, including purely perceptual or evaluative conflicts that require no motor output. Resolving this is critical for refining models of cognitive control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dACC will exhibit a parametric, value-like encoding of the subjective cost associated with resolving perceptual conflict, even in the complete absence of motor response conflict or selection demands.
proposals.method: We will employ a novel perceptual judgment task where participants view stimuli inducing varying degrees of perceptual conflict (e.g., bistable figures, incongruent scene-object pairings) and make non-competitive evaluative judgments (e.g., pleasantness ratings). We will combine fMRI with Dr. Braver's established methods for modeling individual differences in effort sensitivity. Using precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamical systems analysis of resting-state networks to account for individual variation) and generalized linear modeling of trial-wise BOLD activity, we will test for a parametric relationship between dACC activity and both objective conflict level and subjectively reported effort.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence that dACC's cost-encoding function generalizes beyond the motor domain to purely internal, perceptual conflict. It will refine the conflict monitoring theory by dissociating conflict subtypes and strengthen the theoretical link between dACC activity and a domain-general computation of cognitive effort costs.
proposals.novelty: While conflict monitoring is well-studied, systematically probing ACC with tasks that completely decouple high-level perceptual conflict from any motor competition or action selection is a novel and necessary test of its fundamental computational role.

proposals.title: Concurrent Cognitive-Emotional Integration: A Dynamical Systems Model of Effort Cost Valuation Under Load
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research on cognitive control and effort, including Braver's work, often treats emotion as a preparatory state, examining its impact on subsequent task performance. However, real-world cognition occurs amidst concurrent emotional states, creating a critical gap in understanding how effort costs are dynamically valued and neural control is allocated when cognitive and emotional processes must be integrated online. This proposal addresses how the brain computes the subjective value of effort under concurrent load, a core question in cognitive neuroscience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that inducing emotion concurrently with a cognitive effort task will reveal a competitive, resource-dependent integration process, where emotional salience dynamically modulates the neural valuation signal for cognitive effort in the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC), distinct from sequential effects.
proposals.method: We will employ an fMRI-adapted cognitive effort discounting task where emotionally salient images (IAPS) are presented during effortful trials (N-back). Using precision data-driven modeling, we will fit dynamical systems models (e.g., Hopfield networks) to trial-by-trial BOLD activity from the dACC and amygdala to characterize their interaction landscape. Individual differences in behavioral effort discounting will be linked to model parameters (e.g., attractor basin depth) using hierarchical Bayesian regression to identify idiosyncratic integration mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational account of how cognitive control systems dynamically value effort under concurrent emotional load, moving beyond sequential models. It will identify individual differences in integration mechanisms, offering a principled explanation for variability in emotional regulation and cognitive performance under stress.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the canonical task-emotion sequence to model their real-time integration, and in applying Braver's recent dynamical systems and individual-differences approaches to the concurrent cognitive-emotional domain, which has not been systematically explored.

proposals.title: Attention-Based Decoding of Individual Differences in Cognitive Control and Intelligence from Resting-State fMRI Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 neuroimaging research on intelligence and cognitive control has identified broad networks but struggles to pinpoint the specific, dynamic interactions between brain regions that underlie individual differences. Traditional models lack the interpretability to reveal which specific connections or temporal patterns are most predictive. This limits our mechanistic understanding of how the brain's intrinsic dynamics give rise to stable cognitive traits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transformer-based attention mechanisms, applied to time-series resting-state fMRI data, will identify a sparse, interpretable set of cross-regional dependencies and dynamic motifs that are highly predictive of individual scores on cognitive control and intelligence tasks, and that these attention patterns will map onto known effort and control circuits, such as the dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC).
proposals.method: We will adapt a transformer encoder architecture, treating each brain region's BOLD time-series (from a pre-2026 parcellation like Schaefer-400) as an input token. A multi-head self-attention mechanism will learn contextual embeddings of whole-brain dynamics from large-scale datasets (e.g., HCP, UK Biobank). The model will be trained via a multi-task objective to predict validated measures of cognitive control (e.g., task-switching cost) and fluid intelligence. Crucially, the learned attention weights will be analyzed to identify the most influential region-to-region connections for prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, data-driven framework for identifying dynamic neural signatures of cognitive ability with inherent interpretability. It will advance beyond simple activation or connectivity correlates by revealing which specific temporal interactions between regions are most diagnostic. The findings will generate new, testable hypotheses about the neural mechanisms of cognitive effort and control.
proposals.novelty: While transformers are dominant in NLP, their application to model the temporal dynamics of whole-brain resting-state data for cognitive trait prediction is novel. This approach directly integrates Braver's focus on individual differences, effort costs, and dynamical systems with a cutting-edge, interpretable deep learning architecture from a separate field.

proposals.title: Idiosyncratic Neural Dynamics Predict Specific Cognitive Subcomponents: A Precision Modeling Approach
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 cognitive neuroscience has successfully linked brain activity to broad constructs like fluid intelligence (Gf), but these composite scores obscure the distinct cognitive subprocesses (e.g., working memory updating, interference resolution) they comprise. This aggregation limits mechanistic understanding and translational potential, as it remains unclear which specific cognitive costs and control processes are encoded in individual brain network dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in the dynamical attractor landscapes of task-evoked and resting-state networks, particularly in cognitive control regions like dorsal anterior cingulate cortex (dACC), will more accurately predict performance on specific cognitive tasks (e.g., N-back, Stroop) than a composite Gf score, and that these predictive mappings will be highly idiosyncratic.
proposals.method: We will leverage high-quality fMRI data from existing cohorts (e.g., HCP, local datasets) where participants completed multiple cognitive tasks and resting-state scans. Using precision data-driven modeling (e.g., dynamic causal modeling, recurrent neural network models fit to individual subjects), we will estimate subject-specific dynamical parameters (e.g., attractor stability, coupling strengths) within the frontoparietal and cingulo-opercular networks. We will then employ multi-output Gaussian process regression or penalized multivariate regression to predict individual scores on each specific task from these neural dynamical features.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field beyond coarse brain-behavior correlations by establishing a direct, mechanistic link between individual neural dynamics and discrete cognitive subcomponents. It will provide a novel framework for understanding how the subjective costs of specific cognitive efforts are neurally instantiated, offering more precise targets for interventions aimed at cognitive enhancement or remediation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work predicts composite scores, our approach uniquely targets specific task scores using individualized dynamical systems models, directly testing the principle that cognitive control is fractionated and its neural substrates are person-specific, as suggested by Braver's recent work on idiosyncratic mechanisms and effort valuation.

ideas.title: Predicting cognitive control adjustments from pre-trial microstate dynamics
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from meteorology, where short-term weather is predicted by analyzing high-frequency local precursor patterns, to the study of neural dynamics. The analogous problem of predicting imminent system shifts from micro-fluctuations is solved in weather forecasting by focusing on specific, rapidly evolving atmospheric data. The adapted idea is to examine momentary neural fluctuations not as noise, but as forecastable precursor 'microstates.' By identifying specific, fleeting patterns of multi-region micro-scale activity in motivation and control circuits (e.g., in prefrontal cortex, anterior cingulate, and ventral striatum) just prior to a trial, we can test if these patterns reliably predict the direction and magnitude of subsequent cognitive control adjustments on a trial-by-trial basis, moving from correlation to prediction of micro-scale behavioral shifts.

ideas.title: Phasic Noradrenergic Reactivity as a Non-Decisional Gating Signal for Cognitive Control
ideas.description: This research program tests a specific neural mechanism by which cognitive control can be deployed without a cost-benefit decision. It posits that abrupt, stimulus-driven phasic activity of the locus coeruleus-norepinephrine (LC-NE) system, measured via pupil dilation and the P3 component in MEG/EEG, acts as a mandatory gating signal. This signal directly potentiates attentional and control networks in response to salient events (e.g., performance errors, response conflict), triggering adaptive control adjustments reflexively. The design uses pharmacological modulation, computational modeling of reaction time distributions, and neural recordings to dissociate this automatic gating from slower, deliberative control processes that may involve decision-making.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Cognitive Models via a 'Double Dissociation' Framework from Neuropsychology
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the 'double dissociation' method from cognitive neuropsychology to the computational comparison of memory models. The analogous problem is testing between competing theories of cognitive architecture (e.g., single vs. dual-route models). Neuropsychologists solve this by identifying tasks and conditions that produce a double dissociation—where Theory A explains a pattern of impaired/spared functions that Theory B cannot, and vice-versa. We adapt this by constructing a formal benchmark suite of computational and cognitive tasks (e.g., pattern separation efficiency, resilience to interference, speed of episodic binding) and applying targeted perturbations (e.g., simulated lesions, noise injections) to models that avoid versus propose direct hippocampal encoding. The core of the study is to determine if these model classes show a clean, double-dissociated pattern of performance across the benchmarks, which would provide robust, causal evidence for their architectural differences beyond simple goodness-of-fit comparisons.

ideas.title: COMPUTE: Computational Phenotyping of Motivational and Control Deficits
ideas.description: This project uses computational modeling within a unified experimental paradigm to dissect the latent decision processes underlying apathy, fatigue, and cognitive control failures. It tests competing hypotheses by quantifying parameters like subjective effort cost, reward sensitivity, and prior beliefs, moving beyond descriptive symptom clusters. These computational phenotypes are then mapped to specific neural circuits via concurrent fMRI/EEG and autonomic physiology, establishing a mechanistic taxonomy. The outcome is a falsifiable framework that can identify transdiagnostic mechanisms and distinct pathological pathways for targeted intervention.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Modeling to Map Neural Substrates of Decision Variables
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogy from ecology's Species Distribution Modeling (SDM) to neuroscience. In SDM, a species' presence is modeled as a function of multiple environmental variables to define its ecological niche and predict its geographic distribution. The adapted solution is to treat a latent cognitive variable from a behavioral economics task (e.g., the discounted subjective value of a reward in an intertemporal choice) as the 'species' to be mapped. Using neuroimaging data (fMRI) collected during the task, a multivariate model correlates the 'presence' and magnitude of this decision variable with patterns of activity across many voxels (the 'neural environment'). This generates a predictive model of the 'neural niche' for that variable, allowing researchers to visualize its distributed representation and potentially predict decision-related neural states in new contexts or populations.

ideas.title: Modeling the Dynamic, State-Dependent Cost of Cognitive Control
ideas.description: This research program rejects the invariant cost assumption and instead seeks to model the cognitive and physiological variables that determine the moment-to-moment cost of exerting self-control. It employs a multi-method design combining behavioral tasks, subjective effort ratings, physiological monitoring (e.g., heart rate variability, pupillometry), and metabolic manipulation to track how control costs fluctuate with internal resource availability and external task demands. The goal is to develop a predictive model where the net value of a controlled action is determined by a dynamic cost function, not a fixed cost, thereby providing a more accurate and robust account of motivation and effort-based decision-making.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Comparison of Dynamic Threshold Models for Motivated Cognitive Control
ideas.description: This project moves beyond static drift-diffusion models by formally testing competing computational accounts of how decision thresholds change during motivated control tasks. It uses a hierarchical Bayesian framework to fit and compare models with boundaries that collapse, shift, or are set by urgency signals, directly informed by trial-level physiology (e.g., pupil-linked arousal). The winning model will identify the precise dynamic policy governing the speed-accuracy tradeoff under motivation, providing a more mechanistic decomposition of control choices.

ideas.title: Ecologically-Valid Neurocomputational Modeling of Control via Immersive Behavioral Tasks
ideas.description: This approach integrates validated intertemporal and effort discounting tasks into immersive virtual reality environments that provide rich, realistic context for control decisions. During neuroimaging, it collects simultaneous multimodal data: neural activity, choice behavior, and continuous biometric measures of in-the-moment control exertion within the scenario. A hierarchical Bayesian modeling framework then decomposes neural signals into domain-general valuation components and context-specific control implementation processes. This yields a robust neurocomputational model of control that directly tests its generalizability from the lab to ecologically plausible settings.

ideas.title: A Neurocomputational Model of Socially-Embedded Cognitive Control Predicting Long-Term Trajectories
ideas.description: This research program integrates social psychology's nuanced constructs of motivation (e.g., regulatory focus, self-concept, social identity) with neuroscientific models of cognitive control (e.g., conflict monitoring, value-based decision-making in fronto-striatal circuits) to build a neurocomputational model of 'socially-embedded control.' This model is then scaled longitudinally to test its power in predicting individual trajectories of academic and career success. The core synergy is moving beyond static individual differences to model how an individual's neural control mechanisms are calibrated and recalibrated by their shifting social environments, thereby generating testable predictions about critical transition points and long-term outcome divergence.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Comparison of Hippocampal Control vs. Encoding for Spatial Orientation
ideas.description: This research direction will develop a computational model that explicitly contrasts a hippocampal 'control' mechanism for spatial orientation—where the hippocampus modulates path integration and heading updates in other circuits—against classical 'direct encoding' models where place and grid cells constitute the primary spatial representation. By simulating both architectures within identical virtual navigation tasks, the work will generate specific, testable predictions for distinguishing neural population dynamics (e.g., replay sequences, error-related signals) and behavioral phenotypes (e.g., robustness to sensory conflict, learning rates in novel environments) that are uniquely attributable to each proposed hippocampal function. This synergistic approach clarifies the hippocampal role by rigorously pitting competing mechanistic hypotheses against each other.

ideas.title: Disentangling Hippocampal Contributions to the Learning of Familiarity versus Recollection in Recognition Memory
ideas.description: This project tests the specific hypothesis that the hippocampus is not necessary for the learning phase that establishes a stable, perceptually-based familiarity signal, but remains crucial for learning contextual associations that support recollection. We will use longitudinal Neuropixels recordings and closed-loop optogenetic silencing in mice performing a continuous recognition task with parametrically varied object similarity. The key innovation is analyzing hippocampal population dynamics specifically during the initial encoding of objects that are later correctly identified using only familiarity, compared to those requiring recollection. This design directly isolates the representational contribution of the hippocampus to recognition learning, moving beyond the simplistic question of whether it 'encodes' or 'modulates'.

ideas.title: A Control-Theoretic Model of Collaborative Neural Timing in Goal-Directed Teams
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a control-theoretic model where a team is treated as a multi-agent dynamical system. The model formally analyzes how group-level incentives and shared objectives act as control signals that modulate the parameters and coupling of individual hippocampal-cerebellar timing loops. This allows for the prediction of emergent group synchronization in complex tasks, linking the reconfiguration of internal neural oscillators directly to the efficiency of collaborative outcomes. The approach provides a mechanistic bridge from neurocognitive timing mechanisms to quantifiable team performance.

ideas.title: Closed-loop neurostimulation guided by a dynamical model of declarative-procedural memory coupling
ideas.description: This research integrates dynamical systems modeling with real-time neural and behavioral data acquisition to create a testable, adaptive framework. A computational model of the interacting memory systems is continuously updated based on multimodal inputs during a learning task. The system then applies precisely timed neurostimulation to nodes of the hypothesized network to perturb the system dynamics, directly testing causal predictions of the model. This closed-loop, perturbation-based approach transforms the theoretical analysis into an experimentally falsifiable and self-correcting research program.

ideas.title: Dual: A Thermodynamic Model of Cognitive Energetics and Control Failure
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from thermodynamics and statistical mechanics to systematically examine the shared mechanisms of apathy, fatigue, and cognitive control failures. The analogous field models macroscopic properties (like heat and work) by analyzing the probabilistic behavior of microscopic components, using the key concept of 'free energy' to distinguish usable energy from total energy and entropy. The adapted research idea proposes a 'thermodynamic model of cognitive energetics,' where cognitive free energy represents the resources available for controlled processing. Apathy and fatigue are conceptualized as states of low free energy, while control failures are modeled as transitions to high-entropy states where cognitive processes become disorganized. This approach would involve developing formal, quantifiable models to map the dynamics of cognitive resource depletion, dissipation (mental effort/entropy), and predict thresholds for motivational and control failures, offering a unified mechanistic framework.

ideas.description: This research program investigates the coupled dynamical system formed by declarative memory (facts, events), procedural memory (skills, habits), and the motivation-control nexus. By applying time-series analysis and attractor modeling to behavioral and neural data, it seeks to identify characteristic system states (e.g., rigid habit dominance, integrative learning). These dynamical signatures are then linked to longitudinal data on real-world success, testing the hypothesis that specific interaction patterns and their stability predict critical transitions (e.g., skill mastery, career change) and long-term trajectory divergence more accurately than static individual difference measures alone.

ideas.title: Reservoir computing as a substrate for simulating non-reward-based motivational dynamics in adaptive control
ideas.description: This research proposes using reservoir computing (RC) or liquid state machines to implement a model of how non-reward-based motivational systems, such as threat detection or curiosity, engage and modulate cognitive control. The high-dimensional, fading-memory dynamics of the reservoir will serve as a substrate to simulate the internal, time-varying states generated by these different motivational drivers. By training simple readout layers from this reservoir, we can investigate how each motivational context uniquely shapes adaptive timing, decision thresholds, and attentional control, providing a unified computational platform to dissect motivational diversity in a way that static models cannot.

ideas.title: A Closed-Loop Active Inference Model of How Attention and Value Co-Construct Each Other
ideas.description: This research develops a computational model where attentional allocation and motivational value are not pre-defined but emerge dynamically from a continuous interaction loop. The system uses an active inference architecture to simulate how an agent samples sensory cues, generates affective appraisals, and uses the resulting prediction errors to update both its value estimates and its attentional policy. This approach explicitly tests how noise, uncertainty, and competing cues in realistic environments force the cognitive-emotional system to negotiate and construct what becomes 'motivationally valuable' in real time, providing a more fundamental account of focused attention.

ideas.title: A unified hippocampal-cerebellar timing mechanism for predictive spatial orientation
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the hypothesis that the hippocampus and cerebellum form a unified predictive timing system essential for real-world spatial orientation. It posits that cerebellar precise temporal predictions are integrated with hippocampal sequence-based episodic and spatial predictions to control the timing of head-direction updates, path integration, and landmark recognition. The synergy allows the model to explain how an agent dynamically orients itself not just to where things are, but to when spatial relationships will change, enabling fluid navigation in complex, time-varying environments. This challenges the strictly modular view by proposing a core computational loop where spatial learning dictates what needs to be timed, and temporal processing dictates the pace and success of spatial updating.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Niche Construction to Model Cognitive-Emotional Attentional Tuning
ideas.description: This research expands the model of cognitive-emotional interactions in attention by drawing an analogy from evolutionary ecology's niche construction theory. The analogous problem of understanding how organisms and environments co-evolve through reciprocal modification is solved by modeling dynamic feedback loops. We adapt this solution to propose that motivationally valued cues trigger an emotional response that actively constructs a temporary 'attentional niche,' reshaping the cognitive landscape to prioritize related information. The adapted research idea is to formally model this cognitive-emotional niche construction as a reciprocal feedback loop. The model will detail how an emotional signal focuses attentional resources, and how the consequent cognitive processing (e.g., elaboration, prediction) feeds back to modulate the emotional state, thereby refining the ongoing and future attentional focus on motivationally relevant cues.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Model Classes: Testing Falsifiable Predictions on Hippocampal Encoding in Novel Memory Formation
ideas.description: This project moves beyond theoretical comparison by deriving concrete, testable predictions that distinguish models avoiding direct hippocampal encoding from those proposing it. It establishes a computational benchmark using publicly available neural recording datasets to evaluate which model class more accurately predicts observed population dynamics during the encoding of novel experiences. The outcome will be a quantitative scorecard showing the explanatory strengths and failures of each model type, grounded in empirical neural data rather than conceptual alignment.

ideas.title: Applying the 'Centralized vs. Distributed' Network Paradigm to Hippocampal Function in Recognition
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the hippocampus modulates but does not encode representational information for recognition by drawing an analogy from computer networking. In that field, the paradigm shifted from centralized servers managing all data to distributed systems (like P2P networks) where information is encoded across nodes. The analogous solution was to demonstrate that a central authority isn't necessary for encoding representational data like file locations. Adapting this, the research would hypothesize that the hippocampus is not the encoder of recognition information but a coordinator for cortical networks that perform the encoding. Experiments would test if recognition learning persists when hippocampal output is blocked during learning, focusing instead on disrupting its rhythmic coordination or routing functions, thereby redefining its role from a memory encoder to a system facilitator.

ideas.title: Applying Predator-Prey Dynamics to Model Declarative-Procedural Memory Coupling
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from a parallel problem in Theoretical Ecology—the Lotka-Volterra predator-prey model—to analyze interactions between declarative and procedural memory as a coupled dynamical system. The analogous solution uses differential equations to capture non-linear, oscillatory interactions between two interdependent populations. Adapted to the memory context, declarative and procedural memory processes are modeled as interacting entities with mutual inhibition and facilitation, where the 'strength' or 'activation' of one system influences the growth and consolidation of the other. This framework allows for the simulation of learning dynamics, prediction of interference effects (like those seen in sequential learning tasks), and identification of stable equilibria or oscillatory regimes that correspond to optimal knowledge states, providing a formal mathematical tool to explore cognitive phase transitions and training optimization.

ideas.title: Quantifying the explanatory power of cerebellar dynamics for motor execution beyond a binary gating signal
ideas.description: This study moves beyond the qualitative question of whether the cerebellum does 'more than gating' to quantitatively measure how much of motor execution precision it explains. We will record population activity in the cerebellar cortex and nuclei in mice performing a skilled, timed lever-press sequence. A machine learning model will be trained to predict the detailed kinematics of the movement from the neural data. The critical analysis will compare the prediction accuracy of this full model against a severely constrained model that can only utilize a simulated on/off gating signal derived from the same data. A significant superiority of the full model provides a direct, quantifiable measure of the cerebellar contribution to continuous motor control beyond initiating or releasing a motor command.

ideas.title: Adversarial Predictive Modeling Framework for Motivation and Control Theories
ideas.description: This project establishes a formal, pre-registered adversarial collaboration between proponents of competing theories. It develops a multi-method test battery (behavioral, psychophysiological, self-report) designed to elicit divergent, quantifiable predictions from each theory. These predictions are formalized as competing computational models, whose relative predictive power is rigorously assessed using Bayesian model comparison and forecasting on held-out data across multiple studies. The outcome is a cumulative, quantitative evaluation of each theory's scope and precision, forcing theoretical refinement or integration.

ideas.title: Causal multi-scale mapping of valuation circuits to molecular mechanisms in motivational deficits
ideas.description: This research program moves beyond descriptive modeling by establishing a causal, multi-scale pipeline. It first uses computational models of reward valuation to behaviorally phenotype individuals with control disorders, identifying specific computational sub-types of motivational deficit. In animal models and human neuroimaging cohorts, it then employs causal interventions (e.g., closed-loop neuromodulation) on the implicated valuation circuits to test mechanistic predictions and restore function. Finally, it performs high-resolution molecular profiling (e.g., single-cell RNA-seq, ATAC-seq) on targeted neural populations from the same subjects, directly linking dysfunctional valuation computations to specific gene regulatory networks and candidate therapeutic targets.

ideas.title: Adapting Engine Control Unit (ECU) Synchronization to Model Hippocampal-Cerebellar Timing Loops
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from automotive engineering's Engine Control Unit (ECU) synchronization problem to the neuroscience context of hippocampal-cerebellar timing coordination. In modern engines, ECUs use multi-input, multi-output (MIMO) control theory to precisely coordinate the timing of fuel injection, ignition, and valve actuation based on real-time sensor feedback. This analogous solution is adapted by modeling the hippocampal and cerebellar timing loops as interdependent neural subsystems. A formal control-theoretic framework is proposed where population-level neural signals (e.g., hippocampal theta, cerebellar simple spikes) serve as feedback inputs. A central coordination model, acting like a neural 'ECU,' would process these signals to dynamically adjust the phase and coupling between the loops, formally analyzing how optimal timing coordination is achieved and maintained for cognitive-motor integration, despite internal noise and changing behavioral demands.

ideas.title: A Quorum Sensing Model for Emergent Group Cognitive Control
ideas.description: This idea adapts the biological solution of quorum sensing from microbial ecology to explore how group incentives and shared goals modulate cognitive control in team settings. In quorum sensing, bacteria transition from individual to collective behavior upon reaching a density threshold of signaling molecules. The adapted research would investigate if a similar mechanism governs the scaling of cognitive control to the group level, where the accumulation of shared task signals (performance metrics, goal alignment cues) acts as a psychological 'quorum.' The core hypothesis is that surpassing a critical threshold of these shared signals triggers an emergent, cohesive group control state, optimizing team performance in a way that cannot be predicted from individual members' control capacities alone.

ideas.title: Cohort-Robust Causal Modeling of the Motivation-Control Nexus on Life Trajectories
ideas.description: This research program tests the causal, generalizable impact of the motivation-control nexus on long-term outcomes by studying multiple birth cohorts across different sociohistorical contexts. It integrates longitudinal behavioral assessment with genetically informed designs (e.g., sibling comparisons, polygenic scores) and rich covariate data to rigorously control for confounds. The analysis leverages natural experiments and instrumental variables to isolate the effect of the psychological construct from background factors, ensuring findings reflect robust mechanisms rather than era-specific correlations or shared familial influences.

ideas.title: Adapting SLAM from Robotics to Model Hippocampal Spatial Orientation
ideas.description: This expanded research idea uses an analogy from robotics to clarify the hippocampal role in spatial orientation. In robotics, Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) algorithms are a foundational solution for autonomous agents to navigate unknown spaces by concurrently building a map and tracking their position within it, using sensor fusion to correct errors. Adapting this solution, the computational model of the hippocampus will be developed to explicitly simulate how it performs a biological form of SLAM. The model will detail how hippocampal circuits integrate allothetic sensory cues with idiothetic self-motion information (path integration) to simultaneously construct and refine a cognitive map (the 'mapping' function) and maintain a precise, updated estimate of the organism's orientation and location within that map (the 'localization' function), thereby providing a mechanistic account of how spatial orientation is dynamically controlled and corrected during learning and navigation.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Ecological Trajectory Mapping of the Motivation-Control Nexus
ideas.description: This idea adapts the longitudinal field study methods from ecology to scale up the study of the motivation-control nexus to long-term life outcomes. In ecology, researchers track marked individuals in their natural habitats over years to link early behavioral traits to lifetime fitness. Similarly, this research would establish a large, diverse cohort, assessing their motivation-control profiles early (e.g., in adolescence). Participants would then be tracked longitudinally through their natural academic and career pathways using a mix of periodic self-reports, administrative data (grades, degrees, job titles, income), and key life event histories. This design captures the complex, multi-decadal interaction between individual differences and real-world opportunities and challenges, moving beyond lab proxies to directly link the psychological nexus to objective, long-term success.

ideas.title: Use ecological succession dynamics to initialize and stabilize reservoir states for adaptive timing
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological concept of 'succession'—the predictable, self-organizing progression of an ecosystem from a simple pioneer state to a complex, stable climax community—to the problem of creating effective reservoirs for adaptive timing circuits. In the analogous ecological solution, internal feedback and environmental inputs drive the system toward a robust state with memory of past events. The adapted research idea applies this by designing the reservoir computing model's initialization and training phase to mimic succession. The reservoir's nodes and connections are not started randomly but are subjected to a simulated succession protocol using specific input regimes and internal adaptation rules. This drives the reservoir dynamics from an initial, highly plastic and chaotic state (analogous to a pioneer ecosystem) through intermediate stages to a target 'climax' state that is dynamically stable, rich in transient dynamics, and inherently capable of retaining temporal information. This pre-structured, ecologically-inspired reservoir is then used as the core component for an adaptive timing circuit, where it can more efficiently learn to generate and control precise time delays, rhythmic patterns, and interval timing, as its internal dynamics are already primed for temporal processing and robust to noise.
